Preparing for this talk
has been scarier for me than preparing for LSD therapy. (Laughter) "Psychedelics are to the study of the mind what the microscope is to biology and the telescope is to astronomy." Dr. Stanislav Grof spoke those words. He's one of the leading
psychedelic researchers in the world, and he's also been my mentor. Today, I'd like to share with you
how psychedelics, when used wisely, have the potential to help heal us, help inspire us, and perhaps even to help save us. In the 1950s and 60s, psychedelic research flourished
all over the world and showed great promise
for the fields of psychiatry, psychology and psychotherapy, neuroscience and the study
of mystical experiences. But psychedelics leaked out
of the research settings and began to be used
by the counterculture, and by the anti-Vietnam War movement. And there was unwise use. And so there was a backlash. And in 1970, the US government
criminalized all uses of psychedelics, and they began shutting down
all psychedelic research. And this ban spread all over the world
and lasted for decades. and it was tragic, since psychedelics are really just tools, and whether their outcomes
are beneficial or harmful depends on how they're used. Psychedelic means "mind-manifesting," and it relates to drugs
like LSD, psilocybin, mescaline, iboga and other drugs. When I was 18 years old, I was a college freshman, I was experimenting
with LSD and mescaline, and these experiences
brought me in touch with my emotions. And they helped me have
a spiritual connection that unfortunately,
my bar mitzvah did not produce. (Laughter) When I wanted to tease my parents, I would tell them
that they drove me to psychedelics because my bar mitzvah had failed
to turn me into a man. (Laughter) But most importantly, psychedelics gave me this feeling
of our shared humanity, of our unity with all life. And other people reported
that same thing as well. And I felt that these experiences
had the potential to help be an antidote to tribalism, to fundamentalism,
to genocide and environmental destruction. And so I decided to focus my life on changing the laws and becoming a legal
psychedelic psychotherapist. (Applause) Now, half a century after the ban, we're in the midst of a global renaissance
of psychedelic research. Psychedelic psychotherapy
is showing great promise for the treatment of post-traumatic
stress disorder, or PTSD, depression, social anxiety,
substance abuse and alcoholism and suicide. Psychedelic psychotherapy is an attempt
to go after the root causes of the problems, with just relatively few administrations, as contrasted to most
of the psychiatric drugs used today that are mostly just reducing symptoms and are meant to be taken
on a daily basis. Psychedelics are now also being used
as tools for neuroscience to study brain function and to study the enduring mystery
of human consciousness. And psychedelics and the mystical
experiences they produce are being explored for their connections
between meditation and mindfulness, including a paper just recently published about lifelong zen meditators
taking psilocybin in the midst of a meditation retreat and showing long-term benefits
and brain changes. Now, how do these drugs work? Modern neuroscience research
has demonstrated that psychedelics reduce activity in what's known as the brain's
default mode network. This is where we create our sense of self. It's our equivalent to the ego, and it filters all incoming information according to our personal
needs and priorities. When activity is reduced
in the default mode network, our ego shifts from the foreground
to the background, and we see that it's just part
of a larger field of awareness. It's similar to the shift that Copernicus and Galileo were
able to produce in humanity using the telescope to show that the earth was no
longer the center of the universe, but was actually something
that revolved around the sun, something bigger than itself. For some people, this shift in awareness is the most important and among the most important
experiences of their lives. They feel more connected
to the world bigger than themselves. They feel more altruistic, and they lose some of their fear of death. Not all drugs work this way. MDMA, also known as Ecstasy, or Molly, works fundamentally different. And I'll be able to share with you
the story of Marcela, who suffered from
post-traumatic stress disorder from a violent sexual assault. Marcela and I were introduced in 1984, when MDMA was still legal, but it was beginning also
to leak out of therapeutic circles. Marcela had tried MDMA
in a recreational setting, and during that, her past trauma
flooded her awareness and it intensified her suicidal feelings. During our first conversation, I shared that when MDMA
is taken therapeutically, it can reduce the fear
of difficult emotions, and she could help move forward
past her trauma. I asked her to promise
not to commit suicide if we were to work together. She agreed and made that promise. During her therapeutic sessions, Marcela was able to process
her trauma more fluidly, more easily. And yet, she was able to tell that the rapist had told her
that if she ever shared her story, he would kill her. And she realized that that was
keeping her a prisoner in her own mind. So being able to share the story and experience the feelings
and the thoughts in her mind freed her, and she was able to decide that she wanted
to move forward with her life. And in that moment, I realized that MDMA could be very
effective for treating PTSD. Now, 35 years later,
after Marcela's treatment, she's actually a therapist, training other therapists to help people
overcome PTSD with MDMA. Now, how does MDMA work? How did MDMA help Marcela? People who have PTSD
have brains that are different from those of us who don't have PTSD. They have a hyperactive amygdala,
where we process fear. They have reduced activity
in the prefrontal cortex, where we think logically. And they have reduced activity
in the hippocampus, where we store memories
into long-term storage. MDMA changes the brain
in the opposite way. MDMA reduces activity in the amygdala, increases activity
in the prefrontal cortex and increases connectivity
between the amygdala and the hippocampus to remit traumatic memories
to move into long-term storage. Recently, researchers at Johns Hopkins
published a paper in "Nature," in which they demonstrated
that MDMA releases oxytocin, the hormone of love and nurturing. The same researchers
also did studies in octopuses, who are normally asocial,
unless it's mating season. But lo and behold, you give them MDMA, and they become prosocial. (Laughter) Several months after
Marcela and I worked together, the Drug Enforcement Administration
moved to criminalize Ecstasy, having no knowledge
of its therapeutic use. So I went to Washington, and I went into the headquarters
of the Drug Enforcement Administration, and I filed a lawsuit demanding a hearing, at which psychiatrists
and psychotherapists would be able to present information
about therapeutic use of MDMA to try to keep it legal. And in the middle of the hearing,
the DEA freaked out, declared an emergency and criminalized all uses of MDMA. And so the only way
that I could see to bring it back was through science, through medicine and through the FDA
drug development process. So in 1986, I started MAPS as a nonprofit psychedelic
pharmaceutical company. It took us 30 years, till 2016, to develop the data that we needed
to present to FDA to request permission to move
into the large-scale Phase 3 studies that are required to prove
safety and efficacy before you get approval
for prescription use. Tony was a veteran
in one of our pilot studies. According to the Veterans Administration, there's over a million veterans now
disabled with PTSD. And at least 20 veterans a day
are committing suicide, many of them from PTSD. The treatment that Tony was to receive
was three and a half months long. But during that period of time, he would only get MDMA on three occasions, separated by 12, 90-minute
non-drug psychotherapy sessions, three before the first
MDMA session for preparation and three after each
MDMA session for integration. We call our treatment approach
"inner-directed therapy," in that we support the patient
to experience whatever's emerging within their minds or their bodies. Even with MDMA, this is hard work. And a lot of our subjects have said, "I don't know why they call this Ecstasy." (Laughter) During Tony's first MDMA session, he lay on the couch, he had eyeshades on, he listened to music, and he would speak to the therapists, who were a male-female co-therapy team, whenever he felt that he needed to. After several hours, in a moment of calmness and clarity, Tony shared that he had realized his PTSD was a way
of connecting him to his friends. It was a way of honoring the memory
of his friends who had died. But he was able to shift and see himself
through the eyes of his dead friends. And he realized that they
would not want him to suffer, to squander his life. They would want him to live more fully, which they were unable to do. And so he realized that there was
a new way to honor their memory, which was to live as fully as possible. He also realized that
he was telling himself a story that he was taking opiates for pain. But actually, he realized,
he was taking them for escape. So he decided he didn't need
the opiates anymore, he didn't need the MDMA anymore, and he was dropping out of the study. That was seven years ago. Tony is still free of PTSD, has never returned to opiates and is helping others less fortunate
than himself in Cambodia. (Applause) The data that we presented to FDA from 107 people in our pilot
studies, including Tony, showed that 23 percent of the people
that received therapy without active MDMA no longer had PTSD
at the end of treatment. This is really pretty good
for this patient population. However, when you add MDMA, the results more than double,
to 56 percent no longer having PTSD. (Applause) But most importantly, once people learn that if they don't need
to suppress their trauma, but they can process it, they keep getting better on their own. So at the 12-month follow-up
one year after the last treatment session, two-thirds no longer have PTSD. And of the one-third that do, many have clinically significant
reductions in symptoms. (Applause) On the basis of this data, the FDA has declared MDMA-assisted
psychotherapy for PTSD a breakthrough therapy. FDA has also declared psilocybin
a breakthrough therapy for treatment-resistant depression and just recently approved
esketamine for depression. I'm proud to say that we have now
initiated our Phase 3 studies. And if the results are as we hope, and if they're similar
to the Phase 2 studies, by the end of 2021, FDA will approve
MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD. If approved, the only therapists who will be able
to directly administer it to patients are going to be therapists that
have been through our training program, and they will only be able
to administer MDMA under direct supervision
in clinic settings. We anticipate that over
the next several decades, there will be thousands
of psychedelic clinics established, at which, therapists will be able
to administer MDMA, psilocybin, ketamine
and other psychedelics to potentially millions of patients. These clinics can also evolve
into centers where people can come for psychedelic psychotherapy
for personal growth, for couples therapy or for spiritual, mystical experiences. Humanity now is in a race
between catastrophe and consciousness. The psychedelic renaissance is here
to help consciousness triumph. And now, if you all just
look under your seats ... Just joking! (Laughter) Thank you. (Applause) (Laughter) (Applause) Thank you. (Applause) Corey Hajim: You've got to stay
up here for a minute. Thank you so much, Rick. I guess it's a supportive audience. Rick Doblin: Yes, very. Many of them have also
been to Burning Man. (Laughter) CH: There's some synergy. RD: (Laughs) CH: So, in your talk, you talked
about using these drugs to address some pretty serious traumas. So what about some more common
mental illnesses like anxiety and depression, and is that where microdosing comes in? RD: Well, microdosing
can be helpful for depression, I do know someone that has been using it. But in general, for therapeutic purposes, we prefer macro-dosing
rather than microdosing, in order to really help people
deal with the root causes. Microdosing is more for creativity, for artistic inspiration, for focus ... And it also does have
a mood-elevation lift. But I think for serious illnesses, we'd rather not get people thinking
that they need a daily drug, but do more deeper, intense work. CH: And what about outside
the United States and North America, is this research being done there? RD: Oh yeah, we're globalizing. Our Phase 3 studies
are actually being done in Israel, Canada and the United States. So once we get approval in FDA, it will also become approved
in Israel and in Canada. We're just starting research in Europe. And we're actually going to be training
some therapists from China. CH: That's great. We were going to do an audience vote to see if people felt
like this was a good idea to move forward with this research or not, but I have a feeling I know
the answer to that, so ... Thank you so much, Rick. RD: Thank you. Thank you all. (Applause)

People are fearful very much of whatever happened before is not really in the past. It's going to happen again. And so your amygdala, the part of your brain that is more processing fear, is hyperactive. There's more activity in the in the amydala for people that have PTSD than than average normal people. The other thing about PTSD is that it's hard to separate out what's real threat, what's a not. If you you know people are always triggered by different things. So there's less activity in the prefrontal cortex where we think logically. So you're sort of shortcircuited by your emotions. It's a whole different context. It's not that. Um and then these traumatic memories are never really put into the past. And so the hippocampus, which is the part of our brain that stores memories into long-term storage, there's lower activity in the hippocampus and less connectivity between the amydala and the hippocampus when you have PTSD. Hello, everyone, and welcome. Thank you for joining today's event, Introducing POPLAR, the future of Psychedelics Law, and Regulation. POPLAR is the Project on Psychedelics Law and Regulation, which we launched on June 30th. I'm Mason Marks. I'm a Senior Fellow and the Project Lead of POPLAR. I'm also a law professor at the University of New Hampshire Franklin Pierce School of Law. The Petrie-Flom Center is delighted to bring you this event with generous support from the Saisei Foundation. Before we get to the discussion, I'll mention a few housekeeping items. We welcome audience questions for our panelists, so please submit your questions. Throughout the event, I'll be pulling those questions to pose to our panelists. You may be wondering how do I submit those questions. The best way is to use the Zoom Q&A feature. If you scroll to the bottom of your Zoom window, you'll see a small button marked Q&A. Just type your question there, and I'll be checking that frequently. You're also welcome to submit your questions on Twitter using the hashtag POPLAR. That's hashtag P-O-P-L-A-R. If you do submit a question there, Petrie-Flom staff will be monitoring it, and they'll pull it into the zoom Q&A feature. Ways that you should not try to submit a question include a raise your hand feature on Zoom. We won't be using that today. We've also turned off the chat function so that our panelists can really focus in on the discussion. If you're interested in this event and others like it on health policy, bioethics, biotechnology, and related topics, we encourage you to sign up for the Petrie-Flom Center newsletter and to read our blog, the Bill of Health, which features cutting edge commentary by legal scholars. I want to thank the POPLAR team, especially Laura Chong and Chloe Reichel, for making this event possible. Thank you also to Tim Ferriss and Matt Mullenweg who support POPLAR through the Saisei Foundation. With that, let's launch into the symposium. In about 10 minutes, we'll welcome our keynote speaker, Rick Doblin. But first, we have a very special guest to open the event. US Congressman Earl Blumenauer was a vocal supporter of Oregon's historic Measure 109 which, when approved by voters last November, legalized supervised administration of psilocybin in Oregon. He's also been an advocate for reforming federal cannabis laws, including by removing cannabis from the Controlled Substances List, regulating it, and pardoning federal cannabis-related offenses. While there's a lot of misinformation out there about cannabis and psychedelics, Congressman Blumenauer has been a rational voice for evidence-based legal reforms. That's what POPLAR is about. Educating, addressing misinformation, and supporting science-based psychedelics policies that promote equity and public health. Please join me in welcoming our guest, Congressman Earl Blumenauer. Thank you very much, Mason, for the kind introduction and for the work that you are doing. I'm really excited to have this conversation today and the launch of projects on Psychedelics Law, and Regulation, the POPLAR research initiative. When you combine Health Law Policy, Biotechnology, and Bioethics with the Harvard Law brand, this has the potential to have significant influence in this rapidly changing landscape. It's an area that I've been involved with throughout my public service, starting in the '70s with the decriminalization of cannabis in Oregon. I've continued to work to provide leadership on drug policy reform. I've enjoyed working around the country from Bangor, Maine to Santa Barbara. Part of what gives me such hope is that this movement has not been driven by politicians. It is advocates, it's people in the industry, it's people who have been involved with aspects of medical cannabis for years. I think I was the only politician that was involved with our psilocybin Ballot Measure 109 and an equally significant Ballot Measure 110, where we have drug decriminalization and addiction treatment initiative. We're a pretty blue state, but for reference, that initiative passed by a wider margin than Joe Biden's victory in Oregon. We have powerful examples, particularly the impact of psilocybin that goes back decades. But with the federal government's involvement, it's going to be fun to unpack all of that federal government engagement. But we need more and better research. First and foremost, we need to move beyond the anecdotes that so many of us find compelling when there are real-life examples of people we know have experienced it. But it needs more scientific rigor. We need to fight the hypocrisy with the challenge psychedelics face as legitimate medical treatment, face the old obstacles to research despite being less addictive and having fewer health risks than other drugs freely available, nicotine and alcohol, for instance. The contrast with opioids is stunning. That epidemic has killed thousands, damaged millions of lives, seemingly with few meaningful controls. As I mentioned, I think our advantage is that the American public is ahead of the legislature and other policymakers. They are demonstrating their interest and their support, not just according to public opinion polls, but that support is very powerful on the ground. It's just as advocates are demanding an end to the dated and racist approach. It's responding to broad public support as witnessed by those election results. Here in Oregon, the voters were very busy last year, not just with the psilocybin therapy, a measured and controlled approach, but the Ballot Measure that essentially decriminalized hard drugs and invested much more significant resources for drug treatment here in Oregon using the increased revenues from our marijuana legalization. The measure passed access to psilocybin, established a framework and a law to allow for supervised administration of psilocybin-assisted therapy throughout the state. I think this is a major equity issue. One of the challenges we've had with our failed war on drugs and its misapplication is how it has negatively impacted minorities and marginalized groups. The pandemic has revealed health disparities. We know the disparities in terms of the application of the law. Our psilocybin therapy will allow access to communities historically denied full access to traditional forms of medicine. This is especially important for low-income, communities of color, especially veterans who've tried and failed to receive a relief, for example, PTSD, from other forms of treatment. Many Oregonians feel they could benefit from psilocybin-assisted therapy. It is an issue of equity. It's an issue expanding the toolkit. It's responding to science and the public needs. I'm particularly interested in the potential to unlock revolutionary treatments on mood, anxiety, and substance abuse disorders. This could not come at a more important time. The pandemic not only heightened the disparities in the treatment of health care, but it's had a dramatic impact on the mental and emotional health of Americans. Over two in five adults show symptoms of anxiety or depressive disorders. Here in Oregon, we've seen a major increase in overdose deaths. As we struggle to get through the COVID pandemic, we've got a mental health epidemic where it's clear our approaches have not been adequate. I hope our conversation today in the POPLAR center will help us address critical issues, ensuring equitable access for marginalized communities and, as I mentioned, especially veterans. We want to provide robust funding for additional therapeutic research. I look forward to suggestions for removing red tape and bureaucratic redundancies for drug development and therapy that has dogged us as we work to rationalize our cannabis policies. I want to stress that I think it's very important to respect traditional and sacred use of psychedelics by indigenous communities. Native Americans must not be an afterthought. They have much to teach us, and we have much to respect in that tradition, which has been so abused by the majority of American government. It's a tragic history. This is an important list of challenges. I look forward to your conversation today, the work at the center, and for all of you helping us advance this cause. I promise you, I will do my part, not only assisting in the evolution of the Oregon program, but I plan on bringing this movement to Capitol Hill this year. The same way we develop the foundation for the support of our cannabis work, I want to introduce the results of our work in Oregon and around the country. and I would like your center to be able to help raise awareness on Capitol Hill at this critical time in drug reform to help the federal government catch up to where the rest of America is. I think this is truly the opportunity of a lifeline, and I look forward to working with you to make it a reality. Thank you very much. Thank you so much, Congressman, for your powerful remarks and for your leadership. We're very grateful for you joining us today. [inaudible]. We will now welcome our keynote speaker, Rick Doblin. He's the founder and Executive Director of MAPS, the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies. He founded MAPS in 1986, shortly after the Drug Enforcement Administration added the psychedelic MDMA to Schedule 1 of the controlled substances list. Since then, he's been a leading voice in the destigmatization of MDMA and other psychedelic therapies, working closely with the Food and Drug Administration to make MDMA-assisted therapy and FDA-approved treatment for post-traumatic stress disorder. Thanks to his efforts and others at MAPS, that's nearly a reality. Rick is a graduate of Harvard's Kennedy School, and so it's a pleasure to welcome him home virtually to discuss his important work. Welcome, Rick Doblin. Thank you very much, and thank you, Congressman Blumenauer. it was a very inspiring to hear your opening talk and to how you want to take this further. It's just so exciting to be here with you today and to have this topic, being something that's being looked at in a careful way. So I'm going to talk about psychedelics from legal to illegal to medicines to legal again. Stan Grof has talked about, "Psychedelics are to the study of the mind what the microscope is to biology and the telescope is to astronomy." Stanislav is, at 90 years old now, a leading LSD researcher. We all know that the telescope, at the time, was very controversial. Galileo died under house arrest. Father Bruno who espoused the idea that the Earth wasn't the center of the universe was burned at the stake by the Church. So the telescope was extremely controversial and dangerous for people to look through, but now we just see it as a tool. Psychedelics are, similarly, tools, and they're not good or bad in and of themselves. It's how they're used. We hope that the psychedelics will be seen in this destigmatized way as, really, essential tools for understanding who we are as humans and to healing all sorts of different issues. This is the MAPS logo as interpreted by Alex Grey, an artist. The important thing here is that the hands are to the humans. It's about therapy. The psychedelic background is background, and the human contact, the therapeutic use, is the foreground, and so what we're talking about is psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy. This is a picture of Timothy Leary and me in 1990. This was a conversation at a MAPS benefit. I just asked Tim, with all his advice and all of his experience doing psychedelic research here at Harvard and also in becoming a proponent, what advice did he have for me for trying to make psychedelics into medicines through the government. His response was, "Fuck the government. I am so far past asking for permission for anything, but I'm glad you're doing it." That's where he held up my hands. I felt like this was not the passing the torch of cultural revolution, but passing the torch of scientific research. That was 1990. If you look at this chart of the Web of Science Psychedelic Publication, over about 70 years, what you'll see is a peak in the end of the '60s or early '70s, and that, we all understand, is the psychedelic revolution. It was a major topic of scientific research. The backlash happened, the Controlled Substances Act of 1970, and then things declined. Scientific publications declined over the next several decades, and almost all of those papers were just about the risks of psychedelics. Then it started climbing again after 1990, and that's when the group of people at the FDA who regulate psychedelics took over and switched to a new group who wanted to open the door to research, which they did in 1990 with Rick Strassman during a DMT study, negatively focused on schizophrenia. Maybe DMT, the only endogenous psychedelic, maybe too much of it cause schizophrenia. Then 1992, the FDA formally opened the door to psychedelic research. You can see since then that that has rippled all over the world, and now we are really in the midst of a renaissance of psychedelic research. This was just two years after Timothy Leary spoke to me there at the conference. Initially, this was all done by non-profits. These are the main non-profits. Then just a little bit around a year ago, the first IPO for a for-profit psychedelic came about, and now there's a handful of psychedelic non-profits, but there's hundreds, we think, over 400 for-profit psychedelic companies. This is just a list of some of them [inaudible] and traded on the stock markets. I think there are, really, no fundamental regulatory obstacles, at this point, for doing psychedelic research and no fundamental financial obstacles as evidenced by all these for-profit companies. Since MAPS was founded in '86, we've raised over 130 million in donations and grants for psychedelic therapy and medical marijuana research. But in the last two years, the for-profits have raised about $1.5 billion. That just gives you a sense of the scale of things. This is our corporate structure. MAPS is the non-profit, which I started in April 1986, and the public benefit corporation is our for-profit pharmaceutical arm which we started in December 2014. This is our virtuous circle. The public benefit corporation is for-profit. We're trying to do two different things. One is bring back psychedelic-assisted therapy but also market pharmaceuticals in a way that makes more sense than profit-maximizing. Here, what we do is we maximize public benefit. But the public benefit corporation is 100 percent owned by the non-profits. People donate to MAPS, get a tax deduction. MAPS invests in the Public Benefit Corp. Then the Public Benefit Corp will do the research, will eventually sell MDMA, will make a profit. Profits will be used for the mission of the non-profit. That's where we are currently, whether we can go forward in this way or whether we need to take in investors or so, to globalize, to commercialize, to reach sustainability is not clear yet, but this is our current corporate structure. This is an important regulation that was passed, actually, in 1984, called data exclusivity. Just to say what this is is it's a different patent. I think one of the problems we're seeing is over broad patents in psychedelics, but that's pro forma for pharma industry, but we wish it wouldn't be happening here. But data exclusivity provides incentives for developing drugs that are off patents. That means that no one can use your data for a period of five years. If you do pediatric studies, you've got an extra six months and the FDA is requiring us to do pediatric studies. Then it blocks a generic competitor from applying for 5 1/2 years, and then it takes FDA at least six months to review. There's this period of data exclusivity to provide incentives for developing drugs like psilocybin, like MDMA, like ibogaine, where the drugs themselves are in the public domain. I didn't know about this till 2013. It's very obscure because pharma companies don't develop drugs that they don't have patents on. This data exclusivity is almost never used but it is a law, and it is providing us the opportunity to tell donors if you help us make MDMA into a medicine then we can become a self-sustaining organization. Data exclusivity in Europe is 10 years. Actually, eight years data exclusivity, two years market exclusivity. Now, our public benefit model, first off, is going around the clock. We maximize therapeutic outcomes for each patient, not profit. We share our findings. We're not creating patents. We create an ethical practice model for the for-profits. We're sympathetic with for-profit, we're supportive, but we want to keep them in check. We see that for-profit motive in our health care system has worked at [inaudible] sense. We are very sympathetic with drug policy reform, and that's where I think we're different than a lot of for-profit companies. I think what they do is they misunderstand their business model. They think that, if people have legal access to psychedelics, either through the organization's psilocybin initiative, or just through decriminalization, or eventually legalization, that that will be bad for their business model, especially if we introduced harm reduction, psychedelic peer support, honest drug education, pure drugs to educate the people to be both psychedelic-informed about how to handle it, I think it's actually going to create more people who want to go to psychedelic clinics with trained professionals covered by insurance. But I think there is this concern among the for-profits that either it's bad for the business model or that they should stay away from the controversy. But I think it's a moral imperative to see the counterproductive nature of the drug war and to work on drug policy reform. We also, obviously, within that context, support the use not just for medical purposes, but for personal and spiritual growth, and also addressing conflict between couples, so couple therapy and cultures. We have a project where Israelis and Palestinians are using ayahuasca and MDMA together, and we're trying to study that as a model for psychedelics for a group conflict resolution. This is our public-benefit model. To give you a sense, Sarko is a police officer from outside Boston. He's a psychotherapist, and he's been given permission by his chief to go through our training program to learn how to give MDMA-assisted therapy to other police officers for PTSD. This was my TED talk. The NSA is advertising on my TED talk. We've reached a place of cultural acceptance, and we're about to start research inside the Veterans Administration. I've been trying since 1990 to offer money to the VA to do MDMA-PTSD research, and it's finally starting to happen at the Loma Linda VA sometime this month. Then shortly after, Rachel Yehuda at the Bronx VA is going to test two versus three sessions. Again, these are all funded by philanthropy, but they are taking place, and it's a major turning point. There's no obstacles anymore working at the VA. We've yet to enroll any active-duty soldiers, that's what we're hoping to do soon. That's again, not a matter of regulation. We're going to start group therapy at the Portland VA early next year. We feel just very optimistic about the way things are going. Now, in terms of regulations, we've had a formal dispute resolution that cost us over a quarter million dollars of legal fees and many months with the FDA. We have a protocol to give MDMA to therapists as part of their training. We have one that we're working on. We submitted a modified one, December 2019, and FDA put a clinical hold on it. They said no too risky, there's no benefits from giving MDMA to therapists as part of their training. We're never going to give me permission with this. Give it up. Try something else to train them. Also, they wanted the lead facilitator of our two-person therapy team to be an MD or a PhD. Right now, they just have to be a licensed therapist. The second person doesn't need to have a license, but they want and insist they have bachelor's degree or 1,000 hours [inaudible] experience or in a program to get a license. Then they wanted there to be a physician on-site for every psychedelic clinic, every psychedelic administration where even for Phase 3 we don't have that. We have a physician doing prescribing, screening, and on-call, but not onsite. These are quiescent pills. It took us a lot of therapists or local small private practice therapists. They couldn't afford a doctor onsite. We went through a major major process of this formal dispute resolution, and we appealed the Division of Psychiatry's views up to the Office of Neuroscience and the Office of Neuroscience completely agreed with us. The clinical hold is removed. The lead person just needs to be a licensed therapist, and the physician does not need to be onsite. This was regulation, not a law, but it was something that, again, the FDA is designed to permit these disputes to go to higher levels, which was terrific. It's important to challenge the FDA because they're nervous about approving stuff like this that's new and then getting blamed if anything goes wrong. As far as our Phase 3 results, just again, we've been permitted to move to Phase 3, final stage of research. Our first study was published May 10th in Nature Medicine. The results were outstanding. We had two New York Times articles. Psychedelic Drug Passes a Big Test. Psychedelic Revolution Is Coming. This was on the front page of The New York Times. Scientific American, MDMA Shows New Promise for Trauma, but the Drug Alone is Not a Cure. That's really the key point we're trying to make, and I'm glad that's getting across. It's not the drug, it's the therapy and the drug making. What were the results? We had a very small p-value probability, meaning one in 10,000 chance that our results, the difference between the groups, was due to a random factor rather than the MDMA-assisted therapy. We had a very large effect size. Effect sizes are on the basis of one, meaning one standard deviation. Our placebo is therapy, therapy with an active placebo versus therapy with MDMA. The effect size of MDMA is 0.91, which is very large. The effect sizes for Zoloft and Paxil, which are approved for PTSD, the only drugs, are either some of their studies failed, or they were small effect sizes or on the very low end of medium, but were high and then very high, 2.1 effect size within subjects, when you combine MDMA with the opiates. It's enormous. Replicated results from Phase 2, no site-to-site variability. It wasn't driven by some great therapists at a certain site. We had an excellent safety profile, no increase in suicidality in the MDMA group. One person attempted suicide twice during the study, and one person had such severe suicidal ideation that she checked herself into a hospital to avoid killing herself. Both were in the placebo group. So we met all the criteria for FDA to approve a drug on the basis of one Phase 3 study instead of 2. Now at the same time, a very similar time, FDA approves this Alzheimer's drug, and it can turn into a big scandal for FDA. They had failed Phase 3 studies. They re-interpreted the data, it was marginal effect. It had significant safety problems, and yet it got approved, and we were rejected by the FDA on going forward with just one Phase 3 study, even though we met all the criteria. There is this concern about the controversy, the stigma of psychedelics. There's the concern about FDA regulators not wanting to be criticized if something goes wrong. But again, this is not a matter of law, it's just these are judgment calls that the FDA made, and so we are going to have to go through to the second Phase 3 study. This is a complicated slide, but just to show that the upper is the studies that we require for the NDA, which is New Drug Approval. We anticipate the second Phase 3 study in May of 2022. We'll get the interim analysis, an early read how it's doing, and then it'll be done in October 2022. Then we analyze the data. If it's good, we submit it to FDA, and then we think by the end of the third quarter, FDA will approve, and now DEA will reschedule 90 days later. Now, that used to be a problem. DEA would delay, and there was no way to force them, but Congress rectified that a bunch of years ago, and they passed a law that said that DEA must reschedule within 90 days. They didn't say which schedule goes in, but DEA and the FDA Office of Controlled Substances Act work on that together. But DEA must reschedule. This was a key regulatory problem that law has changed. Now we can say for sure that DEA must reschedule within 90 days. Now, states have to reschedule as well, and we'll have a lot of work. Twenty-five states automatically reschedule, and the others, you have to do various things with their Department of Public Health or whatever they call it, Commissioners of Public Health, or Board of Pharmacies. So there will be a bunch of work once DEA and FDA approve and reschedule, then we have a lot of work to do on the states. We hope it will end up being in Schedule 3 or Schedule 4. I think that's likely. This is another matter of regulation. How is it going to be regulated post-approval? It's called the Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies, the REMS. It's only prescribed by prescribers certified in the REMS program. It means special education for prescribers. It's only going to be 2-4 hours about the health risks of MDMA. But the therapist who would administer it only can be those that have been through our training program or our training program, but that [inaudible] license to schools of psychiatry and psychotherapy that has the content that we agree with, so only trained therapists and only special prescribers, and it's only shipped to the prescribers. It's never given to the patient, so it's not a take-home drug, it's only to be administered under direct supervision, and there are certain kinds of safety screenings that are required. Under this kind of a situation, it's easy for the DEA to say that there's not going to be much drug diversion. Patients never get it to take home. It's only under supervision, and it's only trained people. So I think that this is what we're going to negotiate with the FDA. Again, this is a matter of regulation, not law. There will be the psychotherapy clinics. There will be, eventually, I think over 6,000 of them in America. There's already over 1,000 ketamine Clinics, so we hear. Also, these clinics, people are going to want to be cross-trained, but therapists want to be cross-trained at MDMA, so psilocybin, ketamine, and whatever else gets approved. It's not going to be like, here's an MDMA clinic, here's a psilocybin clinic. I think this is what it's going to look like. This is our goal during this period of data exclusivity to deliver 1 million MDMA sessions. We think it'll be, on average, maybe a two-session model, half a million patients. The bottom line here is the number of therapists that we need to train, and that's 25,000 therapists we're planning to train in the rest of the decade. These numbers do not include group therapy, where that could dramatically increase what we're doing, and the timeline at the top is the therapist capacity. We're just assuming that they only work with psychedelics part of the time rather than all of the time. They could potentially do more and also, we could do more with group therapy. But there's eight or nine million people with PTSD in America. This is really only scratching the surface of the people that need the help, but we do think it should only be used by trained therapists. One of the biggest issues is going to be how do we fully train therapists. The issue there is, we think that therapists should have the legal opportunity to get the drug that they're going to give to patients, taking it themselves as a patient in a therapeutic setting. How do we do that? We cannot knowingly sell as a sponsor for off-label uses. MDMA for PTSD doesn't inherently include giving MDMA to therapists as part of their training. I said we have protocols, but they're very expensive. We have to do all sorts of data gathering. So what we're going to do is propose for the FDA label that the label will say MDMA for PTSD by trained therapists whose training can include one MDMA session. If the FDA agrees to that, then that means that training therapist is unlabeled, and then we can do that without a protocol, and that's what I think we really need to do. You wouldn't go to a meditation teacher that didn't meditate, a yoga teacher that never did yoga. It makes sense for a therapist who want to volunteer. We're never going to make this compulsory. It's always going to be voluntary. We don't think that every therapist who's done MDMA is better than every therapist who hasn't. It's just these therapists will be more effective themselves if they have the experience with the drug that they're going to give to people. This is how we approach it. Again, it's a matter of regulation. This is a really key important argument. Legalization follows medicalization. This is a chart over almost more than 40 years about the American voter views, a Gallup chart on legalizing marijuana. Now the bottom is for the top as against, and you can see it crosses over around 2013 where you get more than 50 percent are in favor of legalizing marijuana. Now, notice here that there's a rise from the very beginning at '69 through the '70s. This is when Jimmy Carter is elected, in '76 on a platform of decriminalizing marijuana, but then you get the backlash, parents movements, then you get Ronald Reagan "Just Say No", starting in 1980. Support, basically, plateaus for several decades. It only starts rising again around 1997. What happened then? California and Arizona passed the first Medical Marijuana Laws. The rise of medical marijuana tracks the rise in support for marijuana legalization. I think that you get people believing or not sure what to believe about all this propaganda from how terrible marijuana is, not to say that it doesn't have risks, which it does, but I think medical use gives people access to stories of people that it's helpful for. It changes their risk-benefit analysis. Also, you see distribution models, the medical marijuana clinics that aren't surrounded by people with machine guns, it's not underground, and then you're making tax money. This is our history for marijuana. I think it'll be similar with MDMA. What we see here is Denver made the mushrooms the lowest priority. They were the first one to do it, and most importantly, Oakland followed Oregon's psilocybin initiative. We're seeing these things spread across America. Then also Oregon decriminalizes all drugs, DC decriminalizes psychedelics. MAPS is doing psychedelic harm reduction as part of our mission to make drug policy reform work. We are consulting with and have a training program for police in Denver about what to do if they see somebody having a difficult psychedelic experience. We're going to do a pilot of about 100. If that works, we're going to sign a contract to do around 3,000 first responders in Denver. We hope to do that in Oregon and elsewhere to really build in our first responders an understanding of a better way to respond. It's just a nice little slide. We've done it at these festivals, but we're also trying to, as I say, do it with the Denver police and others. The way that we're thinking that this will work is that there will be licensed legalization. This is the long-term goal that people will have a license to do drugs. The thing is that, if you misbehave, your license is taken away, and you get punished for your misbehavior. You don't get punished for your state of consciousness. It's your behavior. It's your performance. The reason why I'm talking about this is that drunk drivers can lose their driver's license, but they easily can go to a bar and buy more alcohol, get in their car, and kill people. We need to make it harder for people, if they're drunk drivers, harder for them to get alcohol too. So I think we regulate alcohol too lightly. This is the program that we're talking about in the future for licensed legalization. We think it's going to require a decade of the psychedelic clinics being rolled out for people to hear these stories from people with positive experiences and also to see psychedelic clinics again emerging from the underground, and that will change people's mind. We'll have licensed legalization, I think, in 2035. Now, how do we handle kids? For alcohol, 23 states where alcohol is prohibited to minors have an override for parents where parents can give alcohol to their kids. I think that's the way it should be with psychedelics, that there should be a parental override. If you look at the cultures that have successfully integrated psychedelics, like the Navajo Native Americans, that Congressman Blumenauer mentioned, or the ayahuasca churches. They don't have these age limits. Parents, we are the family values, so we think that this decision should be made by the families rather than by the government. Edgar Mitchell, the astronaut said, "When we see our fundamental unity with the processes of nature and the functioning of the universe, as I so vividly saw it from the Apollo spacecraft, the old ways of thinking and behaving will disappear." This fundamental unity is what people can experience through psychedelics, and I think that will have profound political implications once we realize our intimate connection to nature, to global warming, to the environment, to other species, to other people who look differently than us or have different religions, different races, different gender orientations. I think this fundamental unity, psychedelics will help people experiences. This is why we need mass mental health as our goal, we need drug policy reform, and we also need drug development. It's a lot easier to work with psychedelics than shoot everybody up in space. This is the political theory behind what we're trying to say is the more people we can help experience this fundamental unity, the better off we'll be, and then we hope 2070 psychedelic utopia that we'll have a more spiritualized world. Right now, we're humans, as a group, lemmings going over the cliff. What we're doing with the environment, and warfare, and prejudice, and authoritarianism, this is our idealistic plan. Now, how did this begin? Just to say, MDMA was invented in 1912. It was rediscovered by Sasha Shulgin. It was introduced into therapy by Leo Zeff, the Secret Chief. It leaked out of this to become ecstasy, and that's what attracted the government's attention. They started criminalizing. This is me in the background going in to ask for a hearing in 1984. We won the hearing. The judge said MDMA should be a Schedule 3 drug, but DEA rejected the recommendation. They criminalized MDMA under an emergency basis while the hearing was still going on. That was illegal. They didn't have the authority to do that. People got busted for the first year it got released. Then I started MAPS, I realized the only way it was going to be non-profit drug development through the FDA. Our first protocols from Harvard, from elsewhere, were all rejected. Then it was 1992 that Charlie Grob and I got permission for the first Phase 1 dose-response safety study. This now points to the FDA, again, as being the key factor. It wasn't regulation. It wasn't laws. People interpreted, differently, the data that they saw at the FDA, and they decided to let the research go forth. This is the psychedelic slide paired by the FDA just to indicate again that they're fully behind us. But we don't have federal funding. The Texas legislature has allocated 1.5 million to the Houston VA to study psilocybin, and we're working with them now to allocate another 1.4 million or 1.5 million for an MDMA arm to compare with psilocybin. He's our main ally. He's heard from so many Navy Seals who've gotten help from psychedelics. He put this amendment to the Defense Appropriations Act to help the DOD do this. It didn't get attached, but it's starting to show that we're doing some work. This is where we need help, federal funding. Matt Johnson just announced at Hopkins that, for the first time in over half a century, NIDA, the National Institute on Drug Abuse has given a grant of 4 million over three years to study psilocybin for smoking cessation. Finally, NIDA is getting over its prejudices against saying anything good about illegal drugs. Now, the last thing that has been really bad about regulations and potentially legal obstruction of Schedule 1 research was with marijuana. It was through the NIDA monopoly, which has been in existence since 1968. There's only been one source of marijuana for FDA-regulated research, but it's only for research, not for commercial use. Therefore, in Phase 3 FDA studies, you have to use the exact same drug that would be commercialized. Since you can't commercialize NIDA marijuana, this is how the DEA blocked marijuana from becoming a medicine by the FDA. We have all these states, but they've done that as a reaction in part against the fact that the federal system was blocked. NIDA also provides terrible quality marijuana, the oldest, the driest. It's really bad. I've been involved with lawsuits against the DEA trying to break this monopoly since 2000. We finally got into court in 2005. Again, we won the case, and the DEA once again rejected the recommendation and kept the NIDA monopoly. But that just ended a few months ago. The DEA, finally, it's going to license other people to grow marijuana so that there can be drug development in the United States with domestic product, and you can see the contrast. They're trying to make good quality marijuana and not what NIDA provides. Also, we just got 12.9 million from the state of Michigan for a study of cannabis in veterans with PTSD in 320 veterans. We're getting state funding, not federal funding. Now that this monopoly has ended, there are really no fundamental political obstructions to research with psychedelics or marijuana. As a closing, I'll just say that this is going to be another area of research, or our regulatory that we'll have to deal with is direct-to-consumer ads. This we created as a fun thing. And then if they thought it had particular value, then they would turn it over to a fellow named Leo Zeff, who we had called the secret chief. Now, Leo was about to retire um in the '70s, in the middle '70s, and he was a clinical psych PhD. Um and so, Ann and Sasha had him try MDMA, and he was uh impressed so much that he decided he's not going to retire, and he's going to start using MDMA and training other therapists and psychiatrists in MDMA. And so, that was like around the middle '70s. And they used the code name Adam for it. Now, it kind of gives you this sense of uh primal innocence, you could say, the Garden of Eden, you know, this So, but it also it's a good scramble of the actual name letters, so MDMA sort of has a lot of Adam in there. And they just didn't want to be clear about what the drug was, cuz the fear was that it would be criminalized. And interestingly enough, um a bunch of chemists at MIT at night, students, and others would uh manufacture it. So, a lot of the early MDMA that was used by therapists in the middle '70s to the early '80s came from MIT. So, it was very good quality. The Boston Group, they were known as a lot of made at MIT. So, what happened though was that MDMA escaped from these therapeutic circles and was then marketed by people calling it Ecstasy. So, what a lot of people don't know is that MDMA was a therapy drug before it was a party drug. And it was the party drug nature of it that really got it criminalized. And in April of 1985, Newsweek did the first big article about MDMA. And that was when we were suing the DEA to try to keep it legal for therapeutic use. That because we need to make the change from the inside out. We need to go to heart of the system, you know, the belly of the beast, the FDA, the DEA, and make change there. Let's go through the FDA." So, that started the efforts in the US in starting in 2000. And then it took us 16 years till November 29th, 2016 to do a whole series of international phase two studies, pilot studies with MDMA for PTSD. And we did them in um the United States, Switzerland, Israel, and Canada. And the thing in phase two, phase one is basically safety studies. Phase two is studies in patients, but you're trying to figure out who does it work with, who to include, who to exclude, what are the doses you use, what are your therapeutic methods, what measures do you use, all these different things to to figure out how to really do rigorous scientific research. And because we didn't have that much funding and we had to just go step by step, it took us 16 years to do that. So, 30 years after I started MAPS, from 1986 to 2016, we had the end of phase two meeting. And that's where you present all your data to FDA and it they gave us permission to move to phase three. It It was tremendous, but I knew that we shouldn't go directly to phase three. The FDA has another program called special protocol assessment. And it's an option. Most pharmaceutical companies don't take it because it delays things um up to a year, you know, 6 to 12 months. For us it took us about eight eight and a half months. But it delays things. But what you do is you negotiate with FDA every aspect of the phase three design and all the other studies and data that they want to see and you come up with this package that would generate all the data for review to make it into a prescription drug. And if you get an agreement with FDA, if it Not all these negotiations, not all these special protocol assessments end up with an agreement. But we got an agreement letter. And what that means is FDA is legally bound to approve MDMA for PTSD if these studies generate statistically significant evidence of efficacy and there's no new safety problems arise. The thing for us, because MDMA is such a popular drug under the name ecstasy, tens and tens and tens of millions of people have taken it. We have, you know, 30 plus more 40 years, you know, more than 40 years now of experience with it. And so we know the safety profile. So we feel pretty confident about that. So we got this um breakthrough therapy designation from FDA in August of 2017. In July 2017 we got the um agreement letter and then in 2018 is when we started phase three. And we have to do two 100 person phase three studies. And you know, there's a what's called a um pivotal and then a confirmatory study. So, it took us from 2018 to um 2020 to um finish the first um phase three study and we published the results this year um May 10th in Nature Medicine. And we're also now by the way, we've negotiated with the European Medicines Agency as well. And we're starting to train therapists throughout Europe to do phase three in Europe. The EMA will accept our US data and our phase three studies are in Israel, Canada, and the US. So, we will, if we get permission, get permission in all three of those countries. And then now we're bringing that to Europe as well and also Australia, Brazil, and and other places. We're trying to globalize. So, the results of our first phase three study um were just better than we could have hoped. And there's multiple different ways that you evaluate it, but the first and most you could say the lowest level is is it statistically significant. And so, statistical significance is of the P values, the probability values and is 0.05. So, what that means is like a a nickel on a dollar. If if if there's a one in 20 chance or less that what you found is due to random factors instead of your intervention, then it's called statistically significant. And you need two of those. And so, one in 20 for the first one and one in 20 for the second one, that's really one in 400 chance that if you have two statistically significant studies that it's due to some random factor rather than your intervention. All right. So, our first study was statistically significant. But, it wasn't .05. The FDA has a special program under certain circumstances, you can get approval on the basis of one phase three study. And to do that, you need .001, which means one in a thousand chance that it's random factors rather than your intervention. So, that's even higher than two independent studies, one in 20, one in 20, one in 400 chance. So, if you have a one in a thousand chance that it's random, you can then proceed to talk about getting approval on the basis of one phase three study. What we actually got was .0001. One in 10,000 chance that it's random factors. Now, statistical significance isn't enough because if you have large numbers of subjects in the study, you can get statistical significance for effects that are very small. The more people that you add, the more you you sort of even out random factors. And so, if your effect is kind of small, you can still get statistical significance if you have large numbers of subjects. So, you can have statistical significance, but clinically not very significant. All right. So, there's another measure called effect size, which has been used more and more to kind of equalize across studies, what's the magnitude of the effect. And so, an effect size of one is one standard deviation away from the norm. And so, that's a that's considered a very a large effect size. The drugs that FDA has approved for PTSD, Zoloft and Paxil, had small or barely intermediate effect size. You know, .3, .4, .56 was the effect sizes of and you can also have failed studies. You only need two successful phase three studies, but you can try four or five times. You just need to get two of them. And so, Paxil, I think, had tried three times, and one of them completely failed. Woah. Yeah. So, what we got is an effect and there's two ways to look at the effect size. And it's the more normal way is called placebo subtracted. So, in in traditional research, you know, you give people a drug and you give them a capsule that has no drug in it. And then you compare that. So, you you sort of subtract the placebo effect from your main effect. That's placebo subtracted. The way our phase three studies are designed, um there's no real um placebo in the sense. It's therapy with an inactive placebo versus therapy with active MDMA. So, the placebo condition is therapy and a lot of it. It's 42 hours of therapy with a male-female co-therapy team with two therapists. So, there's a So, the placebo condition is significant in terms of uh the intervention. So, our placebo subtracted effect size, which means that we're subtracting therapy plus MDMA from therapy without MDMA. So, it leaves just the MDMA. was 0.91, a large effect size. But, within the subjects, because it's never going to be just here's the MDMA, it's always going to be therapy plus MDMA, the effect size was 2.1. It's astounding, two standard deviations from the norm. It's just astounding. Now, none of that would matter if you had a terrible safety profile. So, we had a situation where we had one woman try to kill herself twice during the study. And we had another woman that had such severe suicidal ideation that she checked herself into a hospital to avoid killing herself. And as it turned out, both of them were in the placebo group, not the MDMA group. We had nobody in the MDMA group try to hurt themselves. And so we had an excellent safety record from that way. We had a lot There are some safety issues, you could say side effects, that are acute from the the MDA. So muscle tightness is one, sweating is another. Um you know, but the side effects that we did notice were all short-lived, acute from the MDMA. They were fine the next day and they were not serious. So we had a great safety record. And then there's one other thing, which is back to the efficacy. The FDA We had 15 sites. Two sites in Israel, two in Canada, 11 throughout the United States. And so you have to do a statistical analysis about site-to-site variations. So what the FDA doesn't want to see is that most of your phase three subjects come from one or two sites, and these are highly trained, motivated people, and they get great results, but at all the other sites, doesn't work so well, and then that suggests you can't really scale it. So we had no site-to-site variability. Wow. And we think that's in part because of our training of therapists, but we think it's more due to MDMA. So we had every kind of um condition to get approved on the basis of one phase three study. And so we applied to the FDA, and they said no. They said that um we want more safety data, we want to see the second phase three study. But then the irony was a couple after a a months, what happened is the FDA approved a drug for Alzheimer's. People may have heard about that. On the basis of no phase three studies. They had failed phase three studies, but then they did a reanalysis about a subpopulation, and they had a lot of safety problems. So, that has turned into a big scandal for FDA, and it's being evaluated, and and not only that, but it was for um early um you know, Alzheimer's, um but then they approved it for people anywhere on the spectrum, and it was be super expensive, and so, any case, what we're having to do is um finish the second phase three study. And the big reality check for us is going to come in May of 2022. And that's when we have what's called the interim analysis of the second phase three study. A data monitoring committee looks at the unblinded data, and their goal is to only tell me a number. The number is either zero or whatever. And it's about how many people you need to add to get statistical significance. So, it's 60% of the data. So, if you if the number is zero, that means you're fine to get statistical significance, you don't need to add anybody. If you get a number, you need to add the As I said before, that the more people that you get into your study, the easier it is to find smaller and smaller differences. Or they will say, "Give it up. It's futile. You're never going to get statistical significance." So, there's only one other drug that got breakthrough for PTSD by the FDA. A drug called Tonix Pharmaceutical. By a pharmaceutical company, Tonix Pharmaceutical. And as it turned out, um they failed in phase three for futility. They were never going to make it. Um they failed several times in phase three. So, we're the only breakthrough therapy drug left for PTSD. And the study will be done in October of 2022. And then if the results are good, then we apply to the FDA for approval, and it'll take us a couple months to analyze the data, and then it takes the FDA around 6 months to review it. And so we think by the third quarter of 2023, we will have FDA approval assuming the second phase three study goes well. And then by the fourth quarter, DEA has to reschedule. So, normally if it's a drug that's not a illegal schedule drug, FDA says yes, automatically it's available as a medicine by prescription. But because MDMA is a controlled substance in schedule one, the DEA must approve it with must reschedule within 90 days. And so we're trying to work with various experts in abuse liability. So, we think by the end of 2023, we will hopefully have approval in the US for prescription use of MDMA for PTSD. We think Europe and England will be a year or more behind, but they're likely to do that. And then if we have approval from FDA and European Medicines Agency, then almost all the other countries of the world will end up approving it without new data. We just have to train local therapists. So, that's kind of where we're at right now. We're at this um really important moment. We've enrolled 65 of the 100 people in the second phase three study. And I just want to add one other point. So, in phase three in phase two, what we showed, we do the two-month follow-up. So, it's three MDMA sessions one month apart with 12 90-minute non-drug psychotherapy sessions. Three before the first for preparation and to get building what's called the therapeutic alliance for for patients and therapists get to know each other, get to trust each other so that the patient feels safe. And then we have three 90-minute non-drug um psychotherapy sessions for integration after each MDMA session. And then we do the 2-month follow-up, and that's what it's called the primary outcome measure. And that's where FDA looks to compare the groups, but then we've also done a 12-month follow-up. And that's more for the insurance companies to show that it if is it durable or not. Because if you could have great results, but if it fades very quickly, the insurance companies aren't going to pay it for it. So, and also this is labor intensive, and it's more expensive than just giving somebody a pill. So, if we end up doing that um you know, that this idea that we um can show that it's durable, then we're likely to not just get it approved, but get it covered by insurance. Now, that's actually pretty good for on average severe PTSD that's been more than a decade on the average. And all and everybody was treatment resistant in phase two. They'd all had either SSRIs or psychotherapy, and they still had moderate to severe, but on the average severe PTSD. So, 21% was really pretty good. But, when you add MDMA, it was 56%. More than twice as good. And those are people that no longer qualified for a diagnosis of PTSD. But, many of the others had what's called a clinically significant reduction of PTSD symptoms. So, that if we could have given them another fourth MDMA session, maybe they would also be down below the cutoff for being diagnosed with PTSD. All right, all that's great, but what's even greater is the 12-month follow-up data was 67% no longer had PTSD. So, people kept getting better. Not only was it durable, but they kept getting it better. And the key there is because we're training people to heal themselves. What could I possibly tell you about Rick Dublin that you don't already know? In some parallel dimension where Rick and Maps don't exist, we're all still sitting in gloomy faculty sellers, drinking coffee from crack mugs and dreaming of funding. Rick founded MAPS almost 50 years ago and since then has led the charge in establishing legal frameworks for the therapeutic use of psychedelics and marijuana as prescription medicines and tools for personal growth. With MDMA, MAPS is on the brink of being the first ever organization to crowdfund a drug through the entire clinical testing and approval process, requiring something like $2 billion worth of community support. In the wake of last year's shock ruling, Rick is here to talk to us today about the future of MDMA and the FDA. Please welcome to the stage Rick Doblin. >> Wow, thank you. Um the the first is that I want to echo something that Ethan Nadleman said about how Iceland could be a model for the world. And because of its small size and because of kind of the mystical nature of a lot of people in Iceland, but also because a lot of the challenges that they have with alcoholism, substance abuse, depression, that Iceland has the opportunity to be a country that has fully integrated psychedelics throughout its entire system. And I think this might take 10 years or so, but it could be a leading example for what I've been talking about as net zero trauma by 2070. that Iceland, if it can introduce this into the national health insurance, that into the education system, honest drug education, into working with uh recently released people from prison to reduce recidivism, that Iceland has this model opportunity because of its scale and size to be an example for the rest of the world. Um the the other thing that I'd like to say is it um doesn't actually cost $2 billion to make a drug into a medicine. And the reason that they use these numbers is that a lot of that is what's called opportunity cost, which means the pharmaceutical companies are for-profit companies. And the money that they would have invested in other things at a pretty high rate of return is counted as part of the costs of doing drug development. And they also am advertise all the failures into the few successes. So that actually if you have something that you know works and you do a direct line, it can be done for several hundred million dollars. So the $2 billion is used as an example to try to justify the high prices that pharma companies want to charge for their drugs, but it's not actually that cost. And and we have not actually been able to um raise all the money from donors, which I had hoped. I'll explain that a bit more. And what I had thought initially was that when I started MAPS in ' 86 that MDMMA would become a generic drug that it was invented by Merc in 1912. It was in the public domain. That I learned about MDMA in 1982 and it had already been used for quite a few years as a therapy drug. and it was a therapy drug before it became um a party drug. And so the use of MDMA as a tool to help psychotherapy was also in the public domain. And I didn't really understand enough about patents, but I assumed that there was no patents and that MDMA would become a generic. I even did my dissertation on FDA regulation of psychedelics and marijuana in 2001. But it wasn't until 2014, 28 years after I started maps, that I realized that even though I thought I knew everything I needed to know, I had missed something fundamental. Whenever you think you know everything you need to know about something, it's a danger sign. But in this case, what I missed was that there was this obscure um policy that actually had been signed into law in 1984 by Ronald Reagan to provide incentives for developing drugs that are off patent. And these incentives are called data exclusivity. So the the FDA regulatory agencies won't give you patents if you don't actually have original inventions, but they will say that as a pharmaceutical company, you have exclusive use of your data for a certain period of time. The difference is if a patent somebody else does that you can block them but here other companies can develop their own data. You just have exclusive use of your own data. So once I realized that and I realized how much money we were had raised and would continue to need to raise, I thought let's start a public benefit pharmaceutical company that is forprofit but it would be 100% owned by the nonprofit and it would be public benefit but then we could use data exclusivity to charge a higher price than a generic price and with that resources use them for additional research and additional training of therapists and that I thought would be sort of this virtuous circle and that was something that I was able to tell donors if you help us make MDMMA into medicine then we will be self-funding for a period of time. So that's where in 2014 we started the maps public benefit corporation. So I'm speaking to you today not as a representative of LICOS but as the president of MAPS. Now, one of the things that I I've said before about the hands, it's about the training of therapists. And so, MAPS has this program to train therapists around the world. And so there's three basic steps to how we approach the training of therapists. Now, usually we want to work with therapists that have been licensed, have have had experience as therapists. And we know from the traditional um use, indigenous use of Iawaska, of mushrooms, of peyote, that it can take years and years and years to really become a proficient shaman or a proficient um sufficiently skilled person to really help others through psychedelic experiences. So we're not able I think to say because of the scale of the need that we're going to require people to go through years and years of training before they do that. But the first part is they were already trained as therapists. Then the second sort of as a prerequisite or they want to become therapists. Then the next part is that they do the six-day residential program where there's no drugs involved but it's watching videotapes of therapy sessions. It's uh role play. It's understanding our therapeutic method. It's understanding our adherence criteria, our core competencies, and how we have developed the therapeutic approach based on a lot of the work that was originally done for LSD and psilocybin by Stan Grath and others in a transpersonal psychology format. But we want to train people in this approach. And a lot of it is undoing what people have been trained. this idea that you are trying to help people heal themselves and you don't need to intervene as much but you do need to support and support whatever is emerging. The second part of the training is experiential and this is where we get into some challenges with kind of current understanding and regulatory agencies. But the FDA gave us permission for 125 people to go through a protocol to receive MDMA as part of their training in a therapeutic setting. And most of the people that worked on phase two and phase three had their own MDMMA experiences. But it's very important that this be seen as optional. It will never be required that people must have this experience. We want to welcome skeptics. We want to welcome therapists who have either religious or medical reasons why they don't want to do psychedelics and don't want to do MDMA. So we want to strongly encourage but not require. And that's I think a key thing. And then the third part is supervision where once people start working on a PTSD study, we have we do videotape it and then we have adherence raers who watch the videotapes who give notes to supervisors who then speak to the therapist and then we see if they've incorporated that and then they're off on their own. And then post approval, the idea is that the FDA doesn't regulate therapy. The FDA doesn't regulate medicine. So therapists are free to do whatever they want once they've got the basic training. We're not trying to say that therapists must use this method that we've developed. We just want them to know it and then they're free to incorporate it with whatever else that they know. Now, LICOS did something else that I thought was um strategic mistake, which was that we they we had started research in multiple countries in Europe. And so, LOS in the beginning of 2023 decided we don't want to fund any of this stuff anymore in Europe. We're concerned about our resources. we want to focus money on developing our commercialization infrastructure for the US. So they shut down Europe which was really um strategically not wise because regulatory agencies are reluctant to be pioneers. They don't want to be the only one out on a limb doing something. So the more you have regulatory agencies from around the world saying yes, we've approved research, the more um the easier it makes for other regulatory agencies like the FDA to go forward. So the Dutch government saw the shutdown of research in Europe and they had seen the unmet medical need being addressed in ways that had never been addressed before. The lead psychiatrist um in the uh Dutch studies, Dr. Eric Vermaten, was the chief psychiatrist for the Dutch Ministry of Defense, and he's a world expert in PTSD. And so what happened was that the Dutch government said, "We need a state committee that we're going to set up and we're going to look at this situation and decide what are we going to do in the Netherlands." Now, the Dutch state committee in a sense was the FDA advisory committee version in the Netherlands except for a couple main differences. They had real subject matter experts who knew what they were doing as compared if you watch the FDA advisory committee a lot of them like I don't really know anything about psychedelics or I don't really know even about therapy and one woman said why do we even need MDMA because our treatments are great they they work for everybody you know what do we need anything new for so the Dutch state committee issued their report two days after the FDA advisory committee if they had issued their report two days before it might have been a whole different story. So, not only did they have subject matter experts, but they had a year to study all the same data the FDA people looked at for a couple weeks and they had to publish a report justifying their findings. And what the report said is that we believe that safety was proven, efficacy was proven and therefore because of the unmet medical need, we need to move forward with naturalist studies, sort of uh compassionate use studies, but where you gather data, real world evidence.
