All right. So, here is the kind of series of issues that were of concern to the FDA advisory committee. The and functional unblinding, criticizing the therapeutic method, the FDA doesn't regulate therapy, adverse events under reportported, uh participants aren't necessarily safe from therapist misconduct, uh lack of diversity in participants, and bias from prior MDMA experience. These were the sort of accusations the concerns that were raised and I'll I'll sort of go through them one by one. So functional unblinding one of the big things that I did in my dissertation which was in 2001 was to look at the double blind problem. And I know it's very difficult if not impossible. How do you do double blind with drugs that are so strong that they have such an impact that you know you've taken them? And so I did a whole long series of studies uh and and looked at and I thought aha I've got the solution. The solution will be therapy with lowd dose MDMA versus therapy with full dose MDMA. Everybody knows they're going to get MDMA. Everybody has the same expectations. And the challenge is what is the low dose that you use? We know that micro doing doesn't work because it's too low. You're not going to confuse micro doing with a full dose. And if you use doses that are too high that have a lot of therapeutic potential, then you won't tell a difference much of a difference between the two groups. So where is this sweet spot of a drug dose of MDMA that is enough to cause confusion but isn't so strongly therapeutic that it makes it very difficult if not impossible to find a difference between the two groups. So over 16 years of phase two studies what we did is we looked at therapy with inactive placebo with 25 milligrams, 30 milligrams, 40 milligrams, 50, 75, 100, 125 and 150. So we looked at all these and what we discovered to our surprise we did one study with um veterans, firefighters and police officers and one group had 30 milligrams, one had 75 milligrams and one had 125. So what I first want to say is that that we do science but science with a political mindset as well. So in this particular study we added police officers not because we thought we'd get any police officers actually volunteering but we just wanted to send a message. It's for veterans, firefighters, and police officers. And sometimes when you do stuff like that, you're surprised. We actually did have police officers volunteer for the study. But what we discovered to our surprise was that the 75 mgram dose group in this particular study did better than the 125. Now when you randomize it doesn't always make things equal. So the people that had 125 had more depression than the group that had uh 75 milligrams. But what we learned from this study is that this 75 milligram dose group is very effective. I personally, you know, prefer higher doses, but but we we found that it was in a therapeutic context, it was really really effective. So what we also discovered again this was where I was initially wrong in my um dissertation solution was that the lower doses did cause amount of confusion with the fuller doses but they made people uncomfortable and they actually reduced the efficacy of the therapy as compared to therapy with no MDMA at all. So the people that got therapy with no MDMA did better than the people that got these lower doses 25 30 40 50 milligrams and then the 75 milligrams was very effective. So we didn't find a sweet spot of what would be the right dose. Now I'm not saying that this would be the same for psilocybin but this is what we found for MDMA. And so we went to the FDA when we were designing our phase three study. We we spent eight months extra after we were given permission to go to phase three in what's called special protocol assessment process and we negotiated everything and we said here we'll be happy to give you blinding with these lower doses but you're going to make our job easier to find a difference between the two groups because we know that therapy with inactive placebo works even better than therapy with lowd dose and so the FDA themselves decided we would use therapy with inactive placebo versus therapy with full dose MDMA and the first session was going to be uh 70 80 milligrams and then the second and third would be option to go from 80 to 120 and roughly 90% went up to 120 and around 90% kept on the third session for 120 but the FDA itself chose the design that we used but to our disappointment the people that we negotiated with at the FDA left the agency this was in 19 is 200 2017. And so by the time we get around to uh 2024, the people that were running the division of psychiatry were more conservative than the the previous people that we'd negotiated with. And we were already filing formal disputes with lawyer scientists against this division for arguments that we thought that were not um were not being based on science, their their responses. And we've won two formal disputes with this division of psychiatry. So even still, it's amazing when you look at actually the results from the blinding is that there is still some blinding even when you give inactive placebo because people are now in an 8-hour session. They're supported to do really difficult work. They've had a lot of preparation. Everybody gets the same preparation. And so some of them did deep work and there was sometimes confusion between whether they actually got the placebo or the MDMA. So you can see from this that the people that had therapy with placebo um 4.5% were positive they had MDMA but 15.9% thought it might have been MDMA. So you got roughly 20% of these people that think that the inactive placebo was actually or could have been MDMA. And then when you look at it the other way around though, the people that got MDMA, you know, this was uh 78% were sure they had MDMA, 15% thought they had. So any case, the idea is there's still a bit of confusion. So we negotiated with functional unblinding. Um now the therapeutic method critiques the therapeutic method was something that we developed. We call it interdirected therapy and it based on a lot of the work that was done as I said with LSD. All drugs have side effects. And you just balance them with the benefits. So when you have a sense that there's a lot of benefits, you're not like, "Oh, we don't want to hear about the harms." So these were accusations again that I don't think were were accurate. Um but again what the ice here this is the institute for clinical effectiveness research. This is a group funded by the insurance companies to try to figure out what is an appropriate price for a drug post approval. As far as we know, nothing like that happened in the first 16 years and nothing has happened in the eight years since then. But there was this situation that should never have happened. So 40% of the people in our study had prior experience with MDMA or ecstasy outside of therapy and 60% had never had any experience with MDMA. And so what the criticism was is these 40% of people like MDMA and so they're going to have fewer side effects because they like MDMA and they're going to have a better response. Well, as it turns out, that's a valid question to raise. Again, you need these critics. But the response was, "Let's look at the data." And when you look at the data, the 60% of the people that had done uh no MDMA ever before were very were not statistically different from those that had done MDMA before either on safety or efficacy. Um so this was the hypothesis that it was going to be a problem, but it wasn't actually a problem. But the important thing to say that's often missed is that 40% of the people had done MDMA before and they still had PTSD. It's not the drug that is what is really effective. It's the therapy. It's the context. Now, this is not to say that people can't heal themselves from PTSD doing it on their own or with friends in a safe way. They can. And she's done work with octopuses, giving octopuses MDMA and then they're more prosocial. She's done work with mice and you give MDMA to a mouse and it releases oxytocin and that promotes new neural connections in prosocial areas of the brain. So, there's this whole um neuroplasticity and people are thinking maybe if you've got a stroke or if you've had some kind of brain cancer or something like that, maybe you can have a pill that helps you route around the damaged parts of your brain without you getting psychedelic. Which I think is perfectly legitimate to try to Yeah, I mean I think what we need is clinicians need to have all the tools to to help people in all different ways. And some people will want the subjective experience and some people won't. I'll say how my first understanding of non-psychedelic psychedelics happened, which was there's a disorder called cluster headaches. I guess I know cluster headaches. Yeah. Okay. And it's ter it's worse than migraines sometimes. They call it suicide headaches. So, this was in 2003 and there's a group called Clusterbusters that is of cluster headache sufferers and they had discovered inadvertently that if you take psilocybin or LSD, it can get you out of a cycle of cluster headaches and delay the return of the the next cycle. And so they contacted me and said, "We don't want to be criminals anymore. Can you do research?" And I was doing a lot of work at McLean Hospital with looking at the risks of MDMA and other things and they were interested in doing this work with cluster headaches. So, the first thing that they did was get a bunch of people with their medical records and verified they had cluster headaches and then where did they take LSD or psilocybin? Usually it was going to a party or something. And then how did that impact their um their cluster headaches. So, the next step was going to be to do an actual study with LSD or psilocybin. And the people at McLean were like, "Oh, no. Timothy Leary's been here. It's too controversial. We'll only do the research with LSD or psilocybin if it's the last resort. Okay. Only if nothing else works." So, the two researchers, John Halpern here and Torsten Passie at the University of Hanover, were working together on this. And he said, "Well, there is something you don't maybe know about this, but it's called data exclusivity." Mhm. What's that? Data exclusivity was actually signed into law by Ronald Reagan in 1984 Mhm. in the Hatch-Waxman Okay. um, laws about FDA. What it means is that it's incentives for developing drugs that are off patent. Okay. So, you have exclusive use of your data, but another company can develop its own data. So, you don't have a patent, you can't block other people, but if you get a head start, then you can have a monopoly position for a period of time. >> Got it. So, in in uh, 2014 when I realized this, we created the MAPS Public Benefit Corporation, which is cuz I didn't want to do a normal pharma company >> Sure. where you maximize profits. Public Benefit Corporations are a modification of capitalism where you where you prioritize public benefit. And minority shareholders cannot sue the management if you're not maximizing profits because you've got public benefit objectives. >> Yes. And so, the idea was now MAPS people make donations to MAPS, MAPS invests in this public benefit company and we're the 100% owner of it. And once MDMA becomes a medicine, then we're going to be able to have an income stream during this period of data exclusivity, so we won't have to constantly go back to donors and say, "Keep giving us more money and more money." Yeah. So, that was the plan. And then by 2018 and 19, we'd raised all the money for all phase one and phase two and phase three research. So this is going to be the first study ever and it started in screening and enrolling to do direct comparison of individual versus group. And from a statistical point of view um just to educate people a little bit is that when you have two effective therapies, individual versus group um to find a statistical difference between them requires large numbers of people. The the smaller a difference you're looking for, the the larger number of people that you need. Right. Um so what this study in Israel is designed is called a non-inferiority study. It's not looking for one to be superior to the other. It's looking to be that they're more or less in the same range. So it's called non-inferiority. Okay. And because it's not as powerful of a statement, you're not saying one is more Yeah. valuable than the other, more useful than the other, you need smaller numbers of people. So this is 168 people. - I think to understand
what's going on with MAPS and to understand what's going on with MDMA-assisted psychotherapy, it helps to think in
terms of exponential growth. (gentle music) MAPS initially was set up
to take MDMA through the Food and Drug Administration, through the FDA, and make it into a medicine for the FDA. The more that I learned
about what we were doing, the more I realized that we
needed to globalize MDMA. (gentle music) So the first MDMA study that
we were able to do was in 2004, and it took us 12 years, until 2016 to complete the series of six Phase 2 safety and
efficacy pilot studies, where we were looking at various questions about basically
how to design Phase 3. (gentle music) So I think the results that we have are absolutely remarkable. And one of the most important questions is can we replicate this? When we received the
Breakthrough Therapy designation from the FDA for MDMA-assisted
psychotherapy for PTSD, that was in recognition of two things. The first was that there
were large numbers of people that were not adequately treated by the currently available treatments, and also that MDMA-assisted
psychotherapy for PTSD was a novel approach. And the FDA wanted to
really acknowledge that and help us move forward
through the system. We still have a lot of ways to go, the hardest is ahead, which is scaling up to Phase 3 studies for MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD, both in the U.S. and Europe, And if we can do that then we
have to scale up exponentially even more to train thousands and tens of thousands of therapists. But, MAPS is in essence, a change agent using pharmaceutical science
as the point of leverage into our society, but the goals go way beyond that. (gentle music)

Could you please tell us  a little bit about history, how  you got into psychedelics,  what happened in 1986, such  a big step, the heroic event  that you did, and how it has  evolved in these 40 years?  Yes, I'd love to talk about  that.  Well, really, it was when  I was 18 years old in 1972,  after I had read Stan Grof's  book, Realms of the Human  Unconscious, that I had  decided to focus my life on  psychedelics.  And that was two years after  the criminalisation of  psychedelics in the  Controlled Substances Act of  1970 and the shutdown  of psychedelic research all  over the world.  Before that, I had been  educated about the horrors  of humanity.  I'm Jewish.  I was born in 1953.  I had distant relatives killed  in the Holocaust and early  relatives who were trying to  build a country in Palestine.  My great-great-grandmother  moved to Palestine in 1904  in order to die and be buried  on the Mount of Olives,  which happened in 1907.  So I was raised on stories of  the Holocaust and the  creation of the State of  Israel.  And so I just thought, why  are these people trying to  kill me or people like me?  Um, so I had, I was born into  safety and support, but it  was aware that the larger  world was dangerous.  And when I was a young boy  was the Cuban missile crisis  between, um, the US and  Russia, where it seemed like  now the Russians are trying  to kill me.  Yeah.  Potentially.  And it was on the edge of  the Third World War.  Yeah.  And we were told in school  to, they called a duck in  cover, you know, duck under  your desk, cover your head and maybe  you'll survive a nuclear  blast, which was not very  reassuring. But it was now  the world is a very  dangerous place.  And my parents had been  successful, and they told me  that they would give me the  freedom to do whatever  I wanted to do.  And I knew that I would  have enough food and  shelter.  And so, I started thinking  about deeper threats in  a way.  Then what changed was also  now, when I was 17, was the  Vietnam War.  And I was in one of the last  years of the lottery for  soldiers to go to Vietnam.  And I'm like, now my own  country wants to potentially  kill me and kill other people.  So I just started wondering  why all this, what is the  story of humanity, of these  constant wars, of these  constant dehumanizing  others?  And I studied Gandhi and  nonviolent resistance.  And so I decided that what  I would do for my  confrontation with the  Vietnam War is that I would  not register for the draft and  I would be arrested for doing  that.  And then I would go to jail.  And so I thought I'll serve my  country by going to jail and  by costing the system  money.  And that's a completely  nonviolent way to respond.  My parents said, that's okay  with them, but you'll never  be able to be a doctor or  a lawyer, my dad was  a doctor, because you'll be  a felon.  And I thought, okay, I'll  accept that.  I'll have to figure out  something else.  It was a felony?  Yeah, to not register for the  draft, yeah.  And I had a passport,  a driver's license, social  security number, was paying  taxes.  I was on all the possible lists.  So I thought, okay, this is  what I'm going to do,  ended up overestimating the  efficiency of the government  and they didn't even notice  that I didn't register.  But all of that was thinking  like, what am I going to do  with my life?  All these people potentially  want to kill me.  I have my survival set up by  my parents.  And then I started  experimenting with LSD.  And the first time when  I was 17 when I tried LSD, it  was very difficult.  I wasn't very emotionally  mature.  But it made me connect to  a larger world outside of my  life.  And I had been very  disappointed by my Bar  Mitzvah when I was 13,  because I'm the oldest of  four kids.  And I was told a story, this  rite of passage is going to  transform you into a man.  That's what this idea was  that this is a ceremony.  Now you can be part of the,  you count now as part of the  prayers.  You need 10 people in  a minyan to have prayers.  And now you would count  once you’re 13.  But nothing happened.  My Bar Mitzvah didn't  change me.  It was an outer rite of  passage, but it didn't engage  me initially.  But when I tried LSD,  I thought, this is what my Bar  Mitzvah should have been.  Where do I fit in?  Who am I?  What is my connection to  this larger world and to  spirituality?  But I was having all these  difficult experiences  because I had these  intimations of something  bigger, and it was  bringing me to my feelings,  to my emotions.  I was so much up into my  head before.  But I was troubled by my  inability to let go, my fear.  You know, it takes a lot of  courage to let go during  a psychedelic experience, to  open up and to let your  sense of self dissolve.  And I didn't have enough of  that kind of courage.  And so I had a lot of  difficulties.  So I went to the guidance  counsellor at my school.  And this was just one of  those strokes of luck.  He knew Stan Grof.  And he had Stan Grof's book,  Realms of the Human  Unconscious, years before it  was published.  He had a manuscript copy of  it.  And so, reading it, I didn't  trust religion, but I trusted  science.  And so Stan was- so this is  the time when you first  learned about Stan Grof?  Yeah, 1972.  Okay.  And after I read this, the  manuscript copy, it was just,  this felt like science looking  at spirituality in a way.  The beautiful thing about  Stan's work was it wasn't  just theoretical, it was  practical in that it was  focused on therapy.  How do you take this new  understanding of the  unconscious and of people's  connections to something  deeper, but also their  connections to childhood  traumas, to birth traumas,  to confrontation with death?  How do you work with all of  these things to help people  have a fuller life?  So I felt that he had a reality  check  of therapy.  So that other people, I would  contrast this with is John Lilly,  who was an early  psychedelic researcher,  invented the flotation tank.  But he was more interested  in knowledge, and Stan was  more interested in healing.  So it was knowledge and  service of healing.  And I liked the fact in a way  that, um, because it had  been, psychedelics had been  so suppressed by the  government that made me  think, why is Nixon so  scared of psychedelics?  Because of freedom, right?  Because of freedom,  because of questioning  reality.  Therefore, the fact that it  was being suppressed, made  a big difference.  Now, the other part of this is  that I had a certain privilege.  And so Primo Levi is  a renowned Holocaust writer.  He went through the  Holocaust and he wrote. But  he wrote about how people  tend to think that social  change movements, that  revolutions start  with the downtrodden, with  the people that are  oppressed, and they throw  off their chains and then  they struggle for freedom.  But he says that's not  usually the way it happens.  Because the purpose of  oppression is to make it so  you can't protest, you're  always focused just on  survival.  And what he said is actually  it's the privileged, for one  reason or another, that are  usually the ones that start  the freedom movements.  Yes, the ones who do not  suffer that much, who can  give some energy, some  attention to the change.  Yeah.  So I felt that I had this  privilege and that I was just  so lucky in where in this  generational line I was born.  So my great, great  grandfather was a labourer.  Didn't even read or write.  This is in Poland.  My great-grandfather was  a tailor.  Okay.  My grandfather was  a teacher,  left Poland in 1920, and my  dad was a doctor.  And my mother's family had  built some businesses  as well.  So I had this privilege to do  whatever I wanted.  And so I thought now,  psychedelics are connected  to helping us feel connected  to everything, to other  people, and that you realize  that we have more in  common than we have  separating us.  And if you feel that and  know that, it's harder to  dehumanize others.  So I, um, I wrote a letter to  Stan and I said, in 1972, I'd  like to, um, have your  advice.  How do I become  a psychedelic therapist?  Cause I figured since I was  going to be at this point,  I still thought I was going to  be arrested, and I would  never have a normal job, but  I figured I could be an underground psychedelic  therapist.  So at 18 years old, I said, I'm  going to fight for freedom.  I'm going to be an  underground therapist.  And, um,  Stan invited me to a workshop that he gave in  1972 with Joan Halifax, who  he had just been married to.  I did a workshop with Stan.  I did primal therapy.  I did encounter groups.  I did everything that I could  do to elevate my  consciousness.  That was without LSD, right?  Because it was illegal at that  point?  Yeah, all of this was illegal.  Oh, okay.  Yeah, all of it was illegal.  I identified as  a counterculture drug-using  criminal.  So at age 18, that's who I am.  I'm a counterculture  drug-using criminal, but the  arc of my life has to go from  counterculture to culture,  from criminal to legal, but  I've continued to find  psychedelics useful and  marijuana and other things  useful through my life.  And a fair amount of that  has been accomplished.  But it was this  desire to try to make  a contribution to a more  peaceful world that led me  to say, when I'm 18, I'm  going to focus my life on  psychedelics, but I wasn't  mature enough.  And I had the wrong idea  that the more drugs you  take, the faster you evolve.  So I did lots and lots of  drugs, but I got more  confused and  I underestimated the  importance of integration.  Once I realised that, that  I had to get grounded,  I spent, um, 10 years, I dropped out of school and  my freshman year, I spent 10  years building things,  building houses, um,  building a handball court  and getting grounded.  And it took me 10 years, um,  before I was ready  emotionally to, um, put  psychedelics first to start  again, to become  a psychedelic therapist for  others.  So it took me, as I said, like a decade. So then, in 1982, I went back  to college and my first  semester, I went to be with  Stan Grof and Christina for  a month-long workshop at  Esalen in Big Sur.  And that's where I learned  about MDMA.  And when I learned about  MDMA, it was both  a therapy, it was still legal,  and it was a therapy drug  under the name ADAM, but  had also just started to  become ecstasy  used in a public setting.  So I knew that it was  doomed.  Eventually, this was Nancy  Reagan and Ronald Reagan.  And so I learned about LSD  after the backlash.  And now I'm learning about  MDMA before the backlash,  but I know that there will be  a backlash.  So that's where I started  thinking, okay, I can get  more politically involved.  So you knew there would be  backlash because it's just  too good, right?  So for the political powers,  it's not advantageous for  people to have  this kind of loving  experiences that it gives.  Yeah.  And once it was being used  in a public setting, so by the  time that I learned about it,  about half a million doses  had been used in quiet  settings in people's houses  as a therapy drug.  But one of the people that  used it that way, thought,  aha, this is a good drug.  I can make a lot of money.  I'll turn it into ecstasy.  I'll sell it in bars.  And then it's a problem.  And then it was inevitable.  So from 1982, it was like the  advocates for psychedelics  had several years advanced  knowledge.  The government didn't know  about therapeutic use.  They just knew about the  recreational ecstasy.  So we were able to introduce  a variety of people to MDMA,  either to try it or talk about  it.  To prepare them to be  witnesses once the DEA  would move to criminalise  MDMA, which happened in  1984.  And so I had worked with  Brother David Steindl-Rast,  a Roman Catholic monk, and  he had used MDMA in the  monastery to help him  meditate.  And he talked about it to the  media, how MDMA helps  people achieve a state of  mind that monks will spend  their whole lifetime trying to  do.  I worked with a Jewish rabbi  who compared MDMA to the  Sabbath.  And so these were people  willing to speak to the  media.  So we were winning in the  public, the court of public  opinion, and we were  winning in the court, trying  to slow down the  criminalisation of MDMA.  And in 1985, the DEA kind of  freaked out because they  could see we were winning.  And so they emergency  scheduled MDMA while the  hearings were still going on.  And then we won the  hearings.  The judge said MDMA should  be a medicine.  It should be illegal for  recreational use, but it  should be a medicine.  And the head of the Drug  Enforcement Administration  rejected that  recommendation.  And we sued twice in the  appeals courts, won both  times.  Eventually, we lost.  And I realised that the only  way to bring MDMA back  and to bring psychedelics  back was through the FDA,  was to go where the  suffering is, go where the  science is, and so I started  MAPS in 1986.  The most difficult, the  longest way, which is  resistant though, robust.  Yes.  But it was really because  there's so much negative  propaganda that people  have learned about  psychedelics.  I was raised to say that if  you did LSD six times, you  were certifiably insane.  And I believe we were told  that it hurts your  chromosomes, that you're  going to have deformed  babies, that  it's gonna damage your  brain in all these different  ways.  But these things didn't seem  to be true.  But when you can do  science, one of the things  about the media in America,  and I think this is about the  media most everywhere, is  that they are reporting on  scientific advances.  So that we could have  earned media that could be  worth millions and billions  of dollars over time that  we'd never be able to pay for  by doing science and getting  results published in  scientific journals.  I started MAPS as  a nonprofit psychedelic  pharmaceutical company  with the realisation at the  time that it would never be  profitable, that it would be  a generic medicine, that the  only way to do it was  through philanthropy, that  there was no patents on it.  That was my understanding  when I started MAPS in 1986.  I graduated from college in 1987  and I wanted to get a clinical  psych PhD, learn how to do  psychotherapy outcome  research.  But at this point, MDMA had  just been criminalised and  nobody would let me in to  graduate school.  So a lot of times when I'm  stymied, and I don't know  what to do, I decide to think  about it while I'm high on  marijuana.  Yes, it helps.  It really helps.  It opens different  perspectives sometimes.  Yeah.  So under the influence of  marijuana, and I remember  exactly where I was in my  house, where I had this idea  that I saw a pattern in my  life.  So the pattern was I want  too much too soon.  I'm impatient, I want too  much too soon.  And then I realized I want to  do the science, but the  politics is in the way.  So therefore, I have to study  the politics.  And so I had read an  interview with this, about  drug policy,  with this professor, Mark  Kleiman, who was at the  school that I'd never heard  of before, the Kennedy  School of Government at  Harvard.  And he had mentioned in  this article our lawsuit  against the DEA.  So I called him up, and I said,  would you be my mentor?  If I could get into the  Kennedy School, I need help  to figure out how to work  within the system, how to  open the door to psychedelic  research.  And Mark said, yeah, if you  can get in, I would be glad to  be your mentor.  So I did all sorts of work to  try to get in.  I managed to get in.  And that's where I shifted in  my mind from learning to  treat individuals for  problems that they have to  learning to treat a sick  public policy.  So, from individual therapy  to cultural therapy.  So that was the kind of shift.  And so that's where a big  part of MAPS has been about  public education.  Because one of the  statements that  we've seen recently in  the United States is that  politics is downstream from  culture.  Meaning culture will permit  what you can do in  a political sense.  It's also true in a scientific  sense.  So that really changing  culture and finding ways to  influence people.  So that's basically, MAPS is  trying to do science.  Now, I then eventually got  into the master's program.  Then I got into the PhD  program and did my,  I almost had a job at the FDA.  This was terrific.  I applied for what's called  the Presidential  Management Fellowship.  It's for people who want  careers in the federal  government.  It's highly competitive and  they, like 150 people or so  every year get chosen.  And then you go in your  different departments in the  federal government, but  then they pull you out every  couple of months to meet all  the people, special  leadership training things.  And I managed to get that.  It was right around the time  that the Supreme Court  decided that the Native  American church could use  peyote for religious services.  So that was my topic.  I talked about the Supreme  Court case about religious  freedom to use peyote.  And I got accepted.  And I tried to get a job at the  FDA to learn how they work.  And I went through about  half a year of vetting with  different people, layers of  the FDA.  I eventually met the person  in charge of all drug  research at the FDA.  And they were all ready to  hire me.  And I thought, this is terrific.  I'll work there for a couple  years, see how the FDA  works.  And then at the last minute,  the FDA told the DEA that  they were going to hire me.  And the DEA made the issue.  They said, no way.  So I was willing to do two  things that you'll probably  appreciate that were very  hard for me.  The first one was to wear  a suit and tie to work.  So I was willing to wear  a suit and tie to work and  I was willing to give up drugs  for a couple of periods of  years.  So by the DEA saying that  they would refuse to work  with me, and then I couldn't  get the job, the DEA saved  me from giving up drugs.  That's very funny.  This was now 1990.  So I started in 88, graduated  in 1990.  And that's where I realised,  and I'd met the woman  that's my wife now, but she  was just my girlfriend then.  And she was one year  behind me at the Kennedy  School.  And I realized that I needed  more credibility, that  I would go for the PhD.  She said, I don't need a PhD,  I'll just hire PhDs.  And so she's done that.  She's executive director of  a family foundation.  She gives away a lot of their  money and she's hired PhDs.  But for me, it was necessary.  So I got my PhD in 2001, and  I thought I knew, this is also  a danger sign, I thought  I knew everything I needed to  know  about making a drug into  a medicine.  But I missed something  fundamental that I didn't  learn until 2014, 28 years  after I started MAPS.  My dissertation committee  was a fellow who taught at  Harvard Law School, but was  the leading pharmaceutical  lawyer in America, used to  be the FDA lawyer.  My dissertation was all  about how to work through  the FDA.  And what I learned about in  a way accidentally in 2014,  was that there was  a program that pharma  companies never used.  That's why I was never  talked about it.  But it was in 1984, two years  before I started MAPS,  Ronald Reagan signed into  law a bill that would provide  incentives for developing  drugs that were off patent.  And these incentives are  called data exclusivity.  So what it means is that you  have exclusive use of your  own data to make a drug  into a medicine for five  years, during which there  can be  no competition, unless  another company develops  its own data.  So that's how it's different  from a patent.  If you've got a patent, you  own something.  But if you don't have  a patent, you have use of your  own data.  And then you get an  additional six months of  data exclusivity if you do  pediatric studies.  So that's five and a half  years.  And then it takes the FDA  around six months or more  to figure out, to review  a generic application.  So once I realised this and  data exclusivity is 10 years  in Europe.  So in the Czech Republic, it's  10 years for EMA.  But I realised now there's  a way to make money from  selling MDMA.  And I could tell a different  story to the donors.  The story would be that if  you keep giving us money to  make MDMA into  a medicine, eventually we  become self-funding at least  for this period of time.  And we're not going to have  to keep asking you for more  money and more money.  So,  the idea was now that we  started the public, the MAPS  public benefit corporation,  which was a pharmaceutical  company.  And I didn't want it to be like  a normal company, which is  all about the profits.  But in America, there's the  public benefit corporation,  which means that you  maximise public benefit.  You don't have to maximise  profits.  So in a normal company,  you, the minority  shareholders can sue the  management if they're not  maximising profits.  But in a public benefit  company,  It's declared upfront, so it  makes sense.  Yeah.  So that's what I created.  So the idea was the  nonprofit 100% owns the  for-profit, and then we will  make money during this  period of data exclusivity,  and then with that money,  we'll do global humanitarian  projects, we'll do more  public education, we'll train  therapists, we'll do all these  things.  What happened later was  that  we've raised about  $160 million in donations.  That's decent.  And then we were  succeeding.  We had all the money for the  phase two and even the  phase three studies.  But then we hired the  Boston Consulting Group,  like McKenzie, in a way, to  advise us on the next steps.  And this is where we went  wrong.  So what they said was that,  looks like you're going to  succeed.  But how do you market the  drug?  Pharma companies would  spend 60 or $70 million just  on preparing the market  to a drug.  And they should, if you're  a normal pharma company,  you would have started  years ago to lay this  groundwork.  And so we could not, so the  mistake was I believe the  experts.  And then also we could not  raise that much money from  philanthropy in that short of  a time.  So that's where we started  working with investors.  And then the company  started separating, and the  incentive started separating.  And I said this yesterday,  but I think it's important for  me to say now, is that the  biggest mistake that I made  was that we did not  articulate and quantify what  we mean by public benefit.  And link that to salaries,  stock options, and bonuses.  So that the management of  the company would be  incentivised on public  benefit, not just on profit.  But once we took in  investors and the company  started separating, and we  had a board of directors of  more traditional pharma  people. When we started  trying to put in public  benefit metrics,  it was too late.  It was too late.  And you didn't have control  anymore.  And they didn't have  control, and the company  resisted it.  So I think that was...  So it was bad advice.  It was bad advice that we…  I think when you try to do  something innovative to  apply traditional methods, it  doesn't always work.  And in this case, one of the  traditional methods was  that they  said, as you're getting close,  you've got your data, now  you're starting to negotiate  with FDA for approval, we  have what they call the  quiet period of a year or  more.  And they started not  responding to critics, not  responding to the media.  And so they lost the idea  that we had done, at this  point, 35 years of culture  change that had really been  key to doing the science.  And then by ignoring the  culture, when the critics  come, you need to  continually have a positive  story of what you're doing  and why, and so when the  fear started coming, there was no response. So  that's where I think the cultural narrative changed.  Then the FDA said no. Responding to the media  more than to the science.  Yeah, I understand. And in fact, was it necessary  to search for investors?  Because MDMA, again, it is  that special that maybe the  word of mouth would have  been enough.  Well, it didn't seem that  way.  So when I first started,  I thought, my thought was  that look at all these people  that are doing ecstasy now.  If each of them would just  send me $10.  So unique, right?  Yeah.  I thought then we'll have all  the money we need.  But then I realised that they  would rather use $10 to buy  more ecstasy.  Then send it to me because  first off, they didn't think  we'd ever succeed.  Secondly, they didn't really  care about medicine.  And so we, for the first  15 years, our major funders  were people who were very  comfortable with  psychedelics, but also  wanted to change the drug  war.  And so they saw how do you  deal with this incredible  propaganda?  Actually, when I first met my  wife, when she was, our very  first date,  which she didn't even say  was a date,  we went out for lunch.  She said, we had  a wonderful conversation  about her psilocybin trip, she  had in college that she was  still thinking about.  But then at the end of this,  near the end of this lunch,  she was saying, I just want  you to know I would never  want to have a real  relationship with anybody  like you.  And I'm like, what's wrong  with me?  She said, well, you've done  so much LSD, your  chromosomes must be all  screwed up.  And I'm like, I can't believe  you're saying that.  I said, it may be premature,  but let me defend my sperm.  They're in good shape.  This was our very first  discussion.  But it just goes to show,  super smart people...  That's interesting.  Clearly, she showed very  much interest, right?  Yeah, yeah.  But she believed all this  propaganda.  So, how do you counteract all  that?  And that's through culture  change and through science  and through the media.  And when you ignore that,  the fears come to the  surface.  But my training was all in  how to make it a drug  through the FDA.  I didn't really study  commercialization.  And so that's where I didn't  know what I didn't know.  And that's where I believe  the experts.  And that was the beginning  of the downfall to where we  ended up having people that  now they don't even really  want to be in charge of the  drug plus therapy.  So for me, what public  benefit means is what's best  for patients.  And that means drug plus  therapy because it's not  about the drug.  I mean, that's one of the  main messages, I guess, I'd like people to have is that  it's the context in which you  use something.  These substances, are  like tools, and they can be  harmful, they can be helpful,  but they're not inherently  spiritual necessarily.  It's the safety, the support,  the context, the  understanding, and that's  what really matters.  So the FDA didn't approve  the third phase, but now it  looks like it will finally get  approved?  Yes, I think so.  So they basically didn't say  no, they just said not yet.  And so they want more  information.  And so I think  the information from a new  study will be mostly about  safety, I think, but that's  being negotiated, has been  negotiated.  So my guess is now here we  are near the end of January,  2026.  My sense is that before this  year is over, and maybe  even within the next six  months, the FDA will make  some sort of provisional  approval of MDMA with  some requirement of  additional research.  Exactly what that  provisional approval is,  I don't know, or what the  implications will be around  the world, I'm not sure, but  it will be helpful.  And the company, because  I was really disappointed by  the management, I went  searching for new  management.  And so I found two people  who have now invested in  the company and have  operational control.  One of them is Antonio  Gracias, and he happens to  be one of Elon Musk's  closest friends.  And he was on the board of  Tesla, SpaceX, but he's had  his own growth with  psychedelics.  The other is Sir Christopher  Hahn, who is a hedge fund  person from London.  And I just read an article last  week about all the different  hedge funds, and which ones  made the most money.  And Chris Hahn's hedge fund  was number one, made over  $20 billion last year.  Okay.  Yeah.  But what Chris has done and  what Antonio has done is  they have invested from  their foundations, not from  their funds.  So they're doing this more to  bring this to the world.  They have appointed  traditional pharma people  to run it, which was a disappointment to me, but  nevertheless,  What they've said is that  once MDMA is approved by  the FDA, that it should  immediately be generic in all  of Africa and in India and  possibly elsewhere, and that  they don't want traditional  pharma patent games to  make all these patents to  sue generics to keep it from  going generic, that after the  six years or so of data  exclusivity, they want it to  be generic in the United  States.  And we talked about it a bit  here, coming here, but  a generic drug, from MDMA in  particular costs $5.  Nothing.  The production cost is close  to zero.  Close to zero.  And once you've got the  upfront costs, and you've got  the methods and all that,  the marginal cost is pretty  nothing.  So the potential now is to  bring MDMA as a generic  into most of the world.  And now that the company  is well-funded, it will  negotiate with FDA,  I thought, what is the  purpose of the nonprofit?  What is our goal now?  And that's where I came up  with this new vision. So my parents, and this is  actually a good story, how  I got inspired to do the work  I do.  They sent me to Russia in the  summer of 1970, after my  junior year of high school,  and they gave me Jewish  prayer books, which were  illegal in Russia, and they  said, bring these to the  synagogue if you can.  So they trained me that if  you can't do something big,  these big factors are beyond  any individual, but if you can  do something little, you  should do it.  You still have an obligation  to do something.  It's a responsibility, yeah so  I ended up spending the  summer in Russia, and we  were studying Russian.  There were 60 of us high  school students.  And at the place we went,  there was this young  Russian girl who was  working at the restaurant  where we would eat.  And she and I went for  a walk on the beach one time.  And I was like, I thought you  hate me.  I thought you wanted to kill  me.  I thought all the Russians  are against all the  Americans.  And she's like, no, no, no,  that's not the way it is.  We're suffering under this  government too.  You know, so to see  a Russian whom I was raised to  fear and to see that she was  just another human being, it  just really changed it. And so 1970, we have the  psychedelic renaissance in  America, the psychedelic  culture.  And so the Russians wanted  to buy blue jeans, and they  wanted to buy button shirts  and they wanted to buy  anything that looked like  America.  And so I and two other  guys would collect stuff  from our 60 high school  students, and we'd go sell it  to the black market and  we'd take our cut.  So I made thousands of  rubles, which were  worthless outside of Russia  because they didn't want  anybody to escape with  money.  So I went to the synagogue  and I said, I've got this  prayer books to give you and  I've got all this money to  give you.  And the guy's like, well,  definitely we want it, but  don't give it to us here  because we're being  watched right now. And that was sort of my first  underground activity was  against the Russians at the  height of the cold war.  But also it was this lesson  from my parents that you  need to be an activist.  You need to do something.  You can't just, and so much  we disempower ourselves by  saying, I can't do enough.  But if you can do a little,  that's empowering. Well, one of the essentials of  what MDMA does is it  reduces fear.  Yes.  You know, it reduces activity  in the amygdala, which is  our fear processing part of  the brain.  So people are more open.  They can say deep things,  but it's local.  It's not like a long-term.  Yeah.  I think that what people  often don't see is the  consequences of doing  nothing. Father Bruno was someone  who both experimented  with plant medicines, but  also supported the idea that  the Earth was not the center  of the universe.  And so he was brought  before the Inquisition and  he was sentenced to burn at  the stake.  And what he said to the  Inquisition is one of the  most courageous things I've  ever heard in my whole life.  He said, your fear of  sentencing me is greater  than my fear of being  sentenced. So that is of course, what  happens.  I also learned a lot as I was  growing up from what  happened in the Warsaw  Ghetto. Um this talk is one that I've been working for a long time to to bring to this audience and fundamentally humans have been using psychedelic substances for thousands of years but it's really only recently that they're finding ways to a much more um kind of accepted version in our society both for medicinal and in in less formal circles obviously for recreational use but pretty much all of that advocacy and research that you have been seeing is really come from very few points in the world and one of those is Rick Doblin who spent the last 40 years or or more of his life most of his life really on this problem and so tonight he's going to share that with us. Welcome Rick. Well, thank you very much. I I really appreciate this opportunity to speak with you all today. Um since uh it's the Long Now Foundation, I I really wanted to start with a very long-term perspective. And so, while we're talking about trying to integrate psychedelics into the mainstream, into Western culture, the last time that actually was the case was 396 AD, 1,624 years ago. And that was when the Goths sacked Eleusis and wiped out the Eleusinian Mysteries, which had been going for almost two millennia starting in 1600 BC around then to 396 AD. And the Eleusinian Mysteries were involving a potion that was drunk called kykeon, and they've figured out that this was moldy rye and barley that they used. Um ergot that can produce a psychedelic experience, and that's where LSD was actually derived. Uh it's synthetic, but it came from ergot. So, I think it's just really important to think about the fact that um we're engaged in a sense in something that's uh 3,200 years, and it's been 1,624 years since the psychedelics have really been accepted in full in the mainstream in Western culture. So, that can suggest that when psychedelics really emerged into the culture in the '60s, it was after a very long hiatus, and it was very difficult emergence, and resulted in a terrible backlash, and now 50 years later, um we're emerging from that. So, I think that's the the big perspective that we need to keep. Now, while I talked about psychedelics being sort of used in culture in our Western culture uh over 3,000 years ago, my story my long now begins 50 years ago. You know, as I was growing up, I was very much uh reading. I was born in '53 and raised on stories of the Holocaust and that just terrified me that people could be so cruel to other people and then for me the next step was the Vietnam and that was my own country that was doing things and I was searching for solutions in a crazy world that that's murder all over the place. So, when I was 17 years old, I went to college, New College in Florida and Sarasota, Florida. It was an experimental college and it still is. It's the honors college of the Florida right now and they had all-night parties with psychedelics till sunrise with dance parties with hundreds of people and I thought, "Wow, this is really great." So, I felt that this was an oasis of sanity in a in a crazy world. And lo and behold, I came across the Whole Earth Catalog and this was absolutely pivotal in my development because I read this Whole Earth Catalog and in it was a little report about a book called Programming and Metaprogramming in the Human Biocomputer by John Lilly. John Lilly who invented the flotation tank and then did LSD in the flotation tank as an effort to try to really understand how the brain is working and and this was very inspiring to me. So, I tried to um do LSD in this um sensory deprivation environments. We didn't have a flotation tank, but we did reduce sensory input, things like that. And that got to be very difficult. This was now again after 1970, the Controlled Substances Act, the '60s had crashed and burned, Nixon was elected, the psychedelic research was wiped out and I had a difficult time with my um psychedelic experiences and so I went to the um guidance counselor. This was the the spirit of the time, quest into the unknown. That's why I really felt that um I wanted to do this exploration and I went to the guidance counselor and said, "I I need trouble help with my tripping." And the guidance counselor said, "There's a book I'd like you to read by Stan Grof who's kind of the um world expert on LSD research. He helped start uh transpersonal psychology." The book was Realms of the Human Unconscious uh Observations from LSD research. And in it, Stan said, "Psychedelics are to the study of the mind what the microscope is to biology and the telescope is to astronomy." And I felt very inspired by this book and this is where my life sort of crystallized where I realized that here he was talking about the realms of the human unconscious. He was talking about psychodynamic areas but also about the sort of death rebirth process and then transpersonal states which I felt also had these important political connections. But Stan was wrapping this in a scientific framework. It wasn't a dogmatic religious framework. It was scientific framework. He was using psychedelics in a scientific study of the mind. And it had the for me the very crucial reality check of psychotherapy. Can you use these insights about the mind about the unconscious to actually help people deepen their participation in life? Cuz I thought it's a crazy world and this is a long-term strategy. I felt that um lots of people were doing lots of other uh shorter-term strategies but I felt that this helping people have the sense of connection was the antidote to fundamentalism, to genocide. And that even though the psychedelic research had been suppressed I felt like it was the most important thing that I could be working on and I didn't know if it would succeed or not but it didn't really matter. And so this was the critical point for me where I focused on my life on psychedelics. The first thing I did was I dropped out of college. Um tune in, turn on, drop out. And I felt that I needed to to get grounded and build things. So I built this handball court. Um then I end up building this house with a lot of help actually. I still have this house all these years later. Um this is a big stained glass window inside of it. And this led other people to come to ask me to build houses for them. So, I basically had a decade of getting grounded trying to be balanced so that I could go back to put psychedelics as the forefront. I 10 years later I went back to the same college and I took a month-long workshop in 1982 at Esalen with Stan and Christina Grof and that's where I learned about MDMA. So, the history of MDMA begins in 1912 when it was first synthesized and patented patented by Merck. But, uh they didn't do anything at all till 1927. And Merck did some preclinical studies in animals and found nothing of interest and they decided to just shelf the compound. This is around the time their patent is going to expire. The next we know of MDMA is the Army Chemical Warfare Service MK Ultra looking for mind control drugs. And then Sasha Shulgin, who was an incredible chemist, he started looking for new drugs for psychotherapy and for personal growth and spirituality. And he resynthesized MDMA, experimented with it on himself, and felt that it had incredible potential for therapy. And so, he gave it to Leo Zeff, who was the leader of the underground psychedelic psychotherapy community from the middle '70s to the early '80s, roughly half a million doses of MDMA were used in therapeutic growth settings, mostly by people that Leo had trained. And it was found to be useful in a in a wide variety of conditions, but it leaked out of that community, became sold as ecstasy, and this was at the time of Nancy Reagan and just say no and the rise of the drug war again. And so, it was clear that it was doomed. So, this was an incredible opportunity to gather support to prepare for the defense of MDMA once the Drug Enforcement Administration finally decided to move against it. Um it was in Dallas at the Starck Club where ecstasy really became popular MDMA became popular as ecstasy in dance club setting. Um, George Bush, all sorts of people participated here and this is what attracted the attention of the DEA. So, in the summer of '84 DEA moved to put MDMA in schedule one. They only knew about it as a party drug. They didn't know about it as a therapy drug and so in 1987 when I graduated college, um, I couldn't get into a clinical psych PhD program and so I I decided I don't know what I'm going to do now with my life. I smoked some pot and I thought I'll think about it while I'm high. And I realized I had a pattern which is I want too much too soon. I want to do the pol- I want to do the science but the politics is in the way so I need to pivot and study the politics. So, in '86 I started MAPS as a non-profit. MAPS is the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies. MDMA is the most gentle of all the psychedelics. That's why I felt it would be the first one to make it through the system. For the first 6 years the FDA rejected five different protocols from Harvard Medical School, UC San Francisco, elsewhere and it was just we're just starting to emerge from this period of multiple decades of total repression of psychedelic research. So, in 1992 Charlie Grob and I obtained approval from the FDA for the first phase one dose response safety study for MDMA in humans. So, that was major major breakthrough. And I started working with PTSD with Dr. Michael Mithoefer and also we started first off in Spain to try to do research here. Um, we were having trouble getting permission in the US for a actual patient study. Uh, this study in Spain was the first MDMA PTSD research ever and the tragedy was that it got a lot of media but that alerted the Madrid anti-drug authority and they shut the study down for political reasons. So, then we went back uh to our efforts in the US and we got FDA approval, but we couldn't get institutional review boards to permit us to do a study in the US. And a bunch of these had rejected the protocol. So, I said, "Let me just try one more time." And I noticed that one of these private companies uh that reviews IRBs was called Copernicus and I felt if anybody is sympathetic to uh political religious pressure on science, it would be the Copernicus group and lo and behold they ended up approving the study. We ended up doing a series of phase two pilot studies in the United States, in Israel, in Canada, and in Switzerland. We did a study in uh military veterans, firefighters, and police officers that we published in The Lancet. But, these studies were all done from basically 16 years, from 2000 to 2016. So, we gathered all of this phase two data and at this point, now that we're starting to show benefits, uh neuroscientists started getting interested and this was something that I could have uh very impressed from Stanford, Boris Heifetz and Rob Malenka. The world's populations need more compassion and empathy for one another. The study of MDMA provides one small but potentially important step towards reaching that goal. So, this is a call to action for the world neuroscientists to study MDMA, not just for therapeutic purposes, but for compassion and empathy. So, it gets back to kind of the political reasons that got me into psychedelics in the first place. And what had also been known is that if you have PTSD, um it changes your brain. You have hyperactive amygdala where you process fear, you have reduced activity in the prefrontal cortex, so you're not thinking as logically, and hippocampus where we store memories into long-term storage activity is reduced. So, this is kind of the brain changes that you get from PTSD, MDMA changes your brain also, but in the opposite way. So, it reduces activity in the amygdala. So, things that were fearful before are not so fearful. People talk about self-love, self-acceptance, looking able better able to look at difficult things. MDMA increases activity in the prefrontal cortex, which increases your logical thinking, triggers that might remind somebody of a trauma, noise that instantaneously they go into a fear-based reaction, now they can have a moment to think about it, separate it out, think more logically. And MDMA increases activity between the amygdala and the hippocampus to facilitate the storage of these traumatic memories into long-term, so that they're not always about to happen in people's perceptions. So, PTSD changes your brain, MDMA changes it in the opposite way. Researchers also found and published a paper in 2019 in Nature to show that MDMA stimulates oxytocin, the hormone of love, nursing mothers, connection, and that that opens up this critical learning period, but also it promotes new neural connections. So, you were actually rewiring your brain in prosocial ways. And that can explain why just a few experiences, sometimes just one, can have long-lasting therapeutic benefits. So, after 30 years of MAPS, from 1986 to 2016, after we had all of our phase two data, we went to the FDA for an end of phase two meeting, where you present your data and say, "Can we go to phase three?" Which is the final stage of research you need to prove safety and efficacy. And we presented this data. We said that in the control group, which is therapy without active MDMA, we had chronic severe treatment-resistant people, and 23% of them at the two month after the last experimental session no longer had PTSD. So, this is really tremendous from just from psychotherapy basically by itself and this is comparable with other psychotherapy. So this showed that our control group and our therapy component was actually a good control because we were trying hard and people were getting somewhat better from that treatment. But when you add MDMA to the therapy you get up to 56% more than twice as much. And so this was really remarkable finding but we also did a long-term follow-up one year later and what we showed is that people keep getting better. The two-thirds no longer have PTSD and of the one-third that still do have PTSD most of them had clinically significant reduction of PTSD symptoms. And if we could have given them one or two more MDMA sessions maybe they would also no longer have PTSD. But what this showed is that the experience that people go through is durable and that people learn to process trauma. They keep getting better on their own. So this is fundamentally different than normal treatments for PTSD from big pharma where you get a drug and it's supposed to correct a biochemical deficit and you take the drug every day for months or years or decades. We're just trying to do get to the root of the problem. We're not talking about addressing symptoms. We're addressing root causes and we're trying to do it in a deep intensive psychotherapeutic process to make people free of drugs. We're not trying to make them keep using MDMA. So just to give you a sense of the therapy. This is a roughly two-minute video of one of the veterans that was in our study. He uh two tours in Iraq and his problem was rage. And if they're not willing to do that, the MDMA helps them to do that, but it can't do the work for them. So we feel like we're more like um midwives than anything else and that people have to do the work themselves. So we have found there there's a great video a documentary called Trip of Compassion. And it's about three of our Israeli PTSD patients and their therapy. Now they were all successes, but but some of the times you can see that it's nothing like the video that I showed. They're shaking, they're they're sweating, they're they're terrified, they're they're hiding under their pillows that and their their blankets that that it's it's very very difficult for some people to to to work through to process this. And a lot of times it's somatized so that if you can't um sort of consciously make it things come to people in their pains in their body or or tensions like that. Um and so we've had several people um who had MDMA drop out of our study because they didn't want to work with the powerful emotions that were coming up. And so I think for those people that it doesn't work for, they I would recommend something more gradual like um more um psychotherapy over a prolonged period of time and then maybe they would then be ready. So I think that um it's about the willingness of people to really engage with the pain. And the MDMA helps them, but it doesn't do all the work. People have to be willing, and if they're not, then they won't get better. So, the lessons that we learned in phase two, though, is that we can enroll people in our studies regardless of the cause of PTSD. The SSRIs, Zoloft and Paxil, that are approved by the FDA for PTSD, tend to work better in um women than in men and completely failed in combat-related PTSD. But we can enroll people with PTSD from any cause. We found that low doses of MDMA do enhance the blinding, but they have an anti-therapeutic effect that they they make people more uncomfortable. They they don't reduce the fear, but they activate them. So, low dose wasn't such a good placebo control. We discovered that it's safe in clinical settings and that we had a medium to large effect size, that we were getting really good results. So, in after this end-of-phase two meeting, we went through this long process of negotiating with FDA with called special protocol assessment to design phase three. So, we came to agreement. We have an agreement letter, and that means FDA's legally bound to approve MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD if our studies get statistically significant evidence of efficacy and no new safety problems arise. Um PTSD is is a terrible situation. 6 to 10% of all people develop PTSD in their lifetime. 18 or so more percent of Iraq War veterans develop PTSD. It's higher in conflict zones. And only 40 to 60% of patients respond adequately to available treatments. There's over a million veterans. This is September 2018. Over a million veterans are disabled with PTSD and are receiving disability payments from the VA for PTSD. It's the third most prevalent service-connected disability after ringing in the ears and hearing loss. The VA spends about 15 to 20 billion per year in these disability payments, mostly for young people. It's going to go on for decades. And they spent in 2017 $7 billion on drugs for PTSD. Mostly these are SSRIs. This is a chart sort of effect sizes how strong is the therapeutic effect. The FDA requires you to have two phase three studies that are statistically significant and so Paxil had three studies. One of them failed. Um but they have small to medium effect sizes whereas in our phase two data we had a large effect size. So on the basis of this though, FDA said MDMA assisted psychotherapy is a breakthrough treatment. It's the most important designation for the most promising drugs. And it gets you more meetings with FDA, shorter timelines. In a sense, we're almost partnering with FDA now to see if this research will actually work. Our treatment is 3 and 1/2 months and there are three MDMA sessions, 8 hours long with a male-female co-therapy team. And people spend the night in the treatment center most of the time. And then there's 12 90-minute non-drug psychotherapy sessions with three before the first MDMA session or or this would be the placebo session, the therapy without MDMA. And then three of these sessions for integration after each MDMA session. So you can see from this that it's really therapy that is enhanced by the MDMA. And we have a two-person team. We we prefer male-female team. Doesn't always have to be that way, but we're really trying to optimize patient outcomes. We've got phase three site locations as I said before, 15 of them, two in Israel, two in Canada, and 11 throughout the United States. We've also negotiated with the European Medicines Agency for a series of meetings after we got the special protocol assessment with the FDA and breakthrough therapy from FDA. Then we went to Europe and we obtained approval in uh 12th, 2018 from the European Medicines Agency Scientific Advice Working Party to move forward to phase three in Europe as well. So, we're a couple years behind in Europe than we are in the US. We've got sites picked out in about uh seven countries, 10 locations. So, we we think we're on track to succeed in this first of two phase three studies. If this drug is approved, the FDA will have special regulations on how it's to be regulated post approval. And it's called Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies. So, it's not the drug, it's the therapy. So, the only people that can prescribe this and treat patients are people that have been trained in the therapeutic approach. And it's only administered in clinics under direct supervision. It's never a take-home drug. There's going to be a centralized pharmacy that ships to the prescribers, not to the patients, and there will be certain uh safety criteria. The things that are under the dotted line are things that we may or may not have to do. The FDA might want to impose a lifetime limit of the number of times people can get MDMA for PTSD, and they might want a patient registry. But, the main aspects of this are to say it's fundamentally different than medical marijuana, which is a drug you take at home and do yourself. This is going to be administered only under direct supervision. We have a therapeutic method that's standardized based on a lot of the early work with LSD. Uh the basic belief is that um there's an inner healing intelligence. We support the emergence of what's happening. We don't try to steer people to certain things. We have a training program that trains therapists. And so, we've been running training programs for uh the last 5 and 1/2 years. Uh just going to run through them very quickly just you can get a sense of the the scale of the number of people that we've trained all over the world. Um this was in the Netherlands in Israel, um, Asheville, North Carolina. This We're getting up to the present, Colorado. Uh, we did a training for therapists of color. Um, and we did another one in Asheville. We also evaluate and supervise them as they work with one PTSD patient. Everything's videotaped and our team watches the videotapes and then we have a, you know, a week-long in-person training. So, it it's rather rigorous, but we think it's optional. We don't want to require people to have to do the MDMA. We think that any therapist who's working with patients would be better able to work with MDMA if they knew what it was themselves. You wouldn't go to a meditation teacher that never meditated or a yoga teacher that didn't do yoga, but we don't think we should require it. And we would think the patients should be able to know the difference. So, this is just a fun thing we made up a long time ago. There'll be a diploma that we give to people if they've been through successfully through our training program. And then we'll add these if they've, uh, the rainbows if they've taken the MDMA. Um, to show again further mainstreaming, this is a police psychotherapist, Sarkis Gregorian, who, um, lives not far from me here in Boston, and he's going through our training program to learn how to give MDMA therapy to other police officers with trauma. So, I've always felt that the police are the predator and I'm the prey. Now, I have a lot more sympathy for the police. I understand their job is very, very difficult and that they need a lot of help and therapy. And so, it's just one of the proudest things for me is working with, uh, Sarkis to try to train him to help other police officers. And we think he's only the first of many. Right now, what's going on is that MAPS is a nonprofit pharmaceutical company set up as a nonprofit research and educational organization, and that was started in 1986, uh, by myself. And in December 2014, we created the MAPS Public Benefit Corporation. And that's the vehicle that we're using to do the pharmaceutical drug development, and that's the vehicle that we'll use to market MDMA by prescription, assuming that we do get FDA approval. And so this sort of virtuous circle is people donate to MAPS, get a tax deduction, MAPS invest in the public benefit corp, the public benefit corp is a for-profit. It will conduct the research and then eventually market MDMA and whatever profits are made go back to the nonprofit for the mission of the nonprofit and we'll reinvest them in other research. Um just to give you a sense, we're now about 80 people. The MAPS team has a bunch of functions which are mainly um running the whole operation, fundraising, communications, media, education, events, operations. We do psychedelic harm reduction and we have a small policy and advocacy team as well. Then the clinical research team is led by um a group of women who worked together at Novartis pharmaceutical company, mostly in vaccines, and they happen to like psychedelics a lot more than they liked vaccines. And so they started building out our pharmaceutical arm, and so now it's about 55-60 people in the clinical research team. We've set up the MAPS public benefit corporation, which is for-profit, because what we realized is that selling MDMA for a profit is taxable. We can't do that inside the nonprofit. So we have the for-profit that will make some money from selling MDMA. That That's a story now that we can tell our donors that we're not going to be constantly asking you for money because you're helping us develop an income stream from the sale of MDMA, but the for-profit branch, the MAPS public benefit corporation, is 100% owned by the nonprofit. So now that it we've gotten so far along and it looks like we're going to succeed and it looks like there's an enormous number of people that need this treatment, we've been inundated by investors who are saying, "Well, you know, fork over dollars to you. We just want a cut of the profits." And so, we're telling them no. And what we're saying is that we're trying and this is what we're saying to donors is that we're trying to do two things. The first thing is to pave the way for making MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD into a medicine to be the first in class psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy. But the other thing we're trying to do is build a more sustainable nonprofit that will act as a as a a good example in the field to put the for-profit people in check. Now, I'm going to talk to you about moving into the future. This is a a chart for about uh 50 years of American voters' attitudes towards the legalization of marijuana. And you can see now it's well over 2/3. But there was a major plateau from around uh the middle '70s to the middle '90s. That was the rise of the parents' movement, the anti-drug movement in the late '70s. And then in 1996, they started moving up. And that coincided with California and Arizona in 1996 being the first medical marijuana states. And then as more medical marijuana states came on, more people knew medical marijuana patients and saw that it was uh again a drug that was demonized that wasn't as bad as people had said. And that caused them to question everything and that caused them to start thinking not just about medical marijuana but about legalization. So, I think that same process going to happen with psychedelics that legalization follows medicalization. So, psychedelics have been used for thousands of years in the Eleusinian Mysteries, and I think that they have been used because they permit a certain kind of escape from our ego-bound focus. You know, our our ego is helping us to survive. It's it's um you know, we we have Abraham Maslow talked about the uh need hierarchy. And so, there's um various needs that we have, and we keep focused on that, and and we always have a sense of who we are both for protection and for advancement in the world. And they provide this um I tell you a a good example, a metaphor, for example, is the Copernican Revolution. And and so, I think it's like that with the ego. We think the world is revolving around us, and the psychedelics help us have this experience, no, we're just a small a bit in a larger system, and um what what Stanislav has talked about, I thought it was a beautiful way he said it, is that psychedelics help the ego become transparent to the transcendent. The ego doesn't go away. The ego doesn't die. We need to be recognizing that we're bound by birth and death. We have to do things to survive. We need that ego, but it gets um enlarged, you could say, and we lose track of the bigger picture. And that's where people get depression and meaninglessness. And so, psychedelics have been something that gives people a sense of our participation in the larger cycles of death and birth. Um the other part of it is a uh an important distinction is that in an 8-hour session, which we have our therapy sessions are 8 hours with MDMA, more or less half the time people's eyes are closed, they have um headphones on, they're listening to music, they're having an interior experience, and then the other half of the time without any kind of script or order when they feel it, they come out and they start talking with the therapist. However, with the classic psychedelics, it's more like 90% of the time they're having an internal experience and only 10% of the time they're really engaged in dialogue with the therapist. So with the classic psychedelics, a lot of the therapy, what we think of as therapy, takes place as preparation and integration. And during the MDMA therapy session, a lot more of the therapy takes place during the session itself. And so that led to the two therapist model instead of the one therapist model. So, what we also know is that a lot of the times that people have trauma have people recover. They're resilient. Like a lot of the people are traumatized right now by COVID will be recovering. Those people that tend to develop PTSD and it's not true for everybody, but those people that get PTSD tended to have a series of traumas and often have difficult attachments in childhood. So, the the the two-person model is often in a sense a not only but often male-female model. It's like recreating a supportive uh parental environment that often times people didn't have as they were growing up. It's also the case that you get two different perspectives. It's It's better, we think, but it's not twice as good. So, the big issue then is going to be economics. How do we do it? And so, that's going to get to the credential issues. So, what happened during our special protocol assessment process, which was during 2018, where we negotiated with FDA the the design of phase three, is we got them to accept, and they they accepted it, that the lead person, the lead facilitator, is a master's level therapist. Has to be licensed as a therapist, but the second person doesn't have to have a license. Could be a student working to get a license. Um After that, we negotiated it. Then, along comes two different companies, Compass for for-profit for psilocybin, Usona for non-profit for psilocybin. And then, FDA imposed new requirements on them, which is that the lead person had to be an MD or a PhD, which we feel makes no sense. So, where we want to end up is that the lead person is a licensed therapist, and the second person doesn't need to be a license at all. It could be a massage therapist. It could be a music therapist. It could be somebody who just has a lot of their own personal experience. Or it could be a student. Or it could be another licensed therapist. I mean, eventually, we also want to explore group therapy for economic reasons as well. They only regulate drugs. So, this is the first time that they've got a drug psychotherapy combination. And they don't really know what they're doing.
