Well, one one of the amazing things that we find for people is that MDMA enhances their memory for the trauma. And so, certain aspects of the traumatic memory are so painful, people have submerged them so much that they don't remember a lot of what happened during the trauma. And And so by reducing the fear and the pain of those memories, people are able to remember more of the trauma. So in a way they're bringing to the surface these memories and they're permitting what's called fear extinction and memory reconsolidation. And so what fear extinction is is you get over this patterned fear-based reaction because the MDMA the drug helps you to do that and then you can process the emotions. And memory reconsolidation, what that means is that we we used to think that memory was like taking a book off the shelf. You you have the memory, you you read the book, you put it back. Actually, you take the book from a multiple different places. It's sort of assembled from different parts of your brain. You remember it and then you reconsolidate and you have to reprint the book. And this kind of explains how people's memories are changed over time. But what we're doing is we are swapping out so we're enhancing the memory for the the episode. Episodic memory is enhanced. But through this fear extinction process and through temporarily people being able to put the trauma in the past and not like it's always about to happen, doesn't always come to them in their nightmares, they're able to replace the emotion that's attached to the episodic memory. So the emotion is one of more peacefulness, acceptance, and it's in the past. And so that's how MDMA really can change how people respond to their memories, enhancing and then also swapping out different emotions that are attached to that memory. Thank you. So, the MAPS Public Benefit Corp is about maximizing public benefit. So, the first thing we've assumed is that the the training program could be a business model as well, training therapists. But, we've decided that we should run the training program at our cost because the real public benefit is healing people from PTSD. And the more therapists there are out there, um the more healing there will be done. The next part is the actual sale of the MDMA. And so, um MDMA is off patent, you know, as I said, invented by Merck in 1912. And I saw a lot of struggles over intellectual property for a drug called ibogaine. And it had a use patent for the treatment of opiate addiction. So, I just thought that that was so destructive to the whole movement for ibogaine that I contacted their patent attorney and I asked him to develop an anti-patent strategy so nobody could patent any of the uses of MDMA. Oh, nice. So, so there's no business model that way. Uh unless somebody comes up with some new use that we've, you know, never heard of or that's never been written about. Okay, then there's a certain kind of patents you could have over process patents, over the production of the MDMA, the encapsulation, there's certain intellectual property that's never been done before. We're going to make all that public. So, our main business model is selling MDMA. And the way that that works is that in 1984, Ronald Reagan signed a law to provide incentives for developing drugs that are off patent. And those incentives are called data exclusivity. And so, what that means is that if you're the first to make a drug into a medicine that's off patent, that automatically the FDA gives you 5 years where no one can use your data. You have exclusive use of your data. No one can use it to market a generic. And FDA is now also realizing that a lot of drugs that are approved in adults are marketed to kids without any information. So, they're requiring us now if we succeed in adults to do studies in adolescents with PTSD. And you get an extra 6 months data exclusivity for that. And it blocks the generic manufacturer from applying until that 5 and 1/2 years is over. It takes FDA at least 6 months. So, it's going to be 6 years. In Europe, it's 10 years data exclusivity. So, that's one model, selling MDMA. Then the other main business model is going to be the clinics. Where are these drugs actually going to be delivered? And as I said, they're only going to be delivered by trained therapists under direct supervision. So, you could imagine um networks of clinics. We're we're actually looking at more and more of a model where we want these clinics to be locally owned, not like a franchise. Well, franchise can be locally owned, but we're not trying to MAPS have uh you know, a thousand clinics. They want to know ketamine, they want to know MDMA, they want to know psilocybin, they're eventually going to want to know, you know, DMT, ayahuasca, ibogaine, whatever comes down through the FDA. So, they're they're not going to be like a MAPS clinic. We we will have um certain Everybody that we train will be on a list and they'll be able to um access the drug and treat patients, but they can innovate and they do whatever they want. Um and that's fine. That's you know, the practice of medicine. However, for those people that want to continue to use our treatment manual, we will say they're MAPS-certified. Rick Doblin, Ph.D. MAPS Executive Director
Paving the Way From Banned Drug to Legal Medicine Psychedelics have been used by tens of millions, hundreds of millions of people so we know the risks of psychedelics. We have hundreds of millions of dollars already spent by the governments on the risks and we don't have to do that research again. It's in the public domain. For roughly $10 million focused very narrowly on an ever expanding series of studies looking at safety of, and efficacy of, psychedelics in specific patient populations, it's a reasonable goal. $10 million is not out of reach, because first of it's like $10 million over 10 years. It's not like you need to write a check all at once. So that the goal for a non-profit organisation through donations rather than investments to raise enough money to do the research, it's, it's possible. to reduce experimenter bias. The double-blind is really to make it so that,
you know, you don't get biased results, that you don't find what you want to find. Right. And so what they first have said is what's
called random assignment. That means everybody is similarly motivated
and then they get randomized either to the control group or the experimental group. The other thing they said is that the independent

[Music] I think in terms of um recommendations, I really don't want to say people should do this or they shouldn't do this. you know whether people should try psychedelics or not try psychedelics is really a decision that they need to make themselves. What I can say is that under certain circumstances psychedelics have been tremendously beneficial to me and to loads of people that I know uh most of them are not designated patients but are just looking for personal growth or for spiritual experiences. Um, the most important thing I could say is for people to think about it as a two-day experience and to think about the experience combined with efforts to integrate it into the daily life that it's not just what happens during the acute effects of when somebody is under the influence of MDMA or LSD or IAS or peyote, although that's very important to structure that as good as possible, but it's about what you bring back and what you really can incorporate and learn from. And so a lot of us, our lives are so hectic. we're working and we're moving and we're traveling and we're doing stuff that the idea of taking time out to do a psychedelic even that is hard for people with LSD really you should be taking 8 10 12 hours but then to think about it as a two-day experience where the next day is for rest is for recuperation is for thinking about what happened um drawing it out in art or doing any number of different kind of techniques thinking about what happens with your dreams you know journaling ing about it, talking to other people about it. The idea of using the second day for purposes of reflection and integration, I think is is really important. Um, one of the other main things to recommend as an approach for those people that do want to do it is that the recreational use, it's kind of looking at using these drugs for just for fun. And I think when somebody is even for recreational users, they should realize that once they catalyze this process of this emerging material from their unconscious to their conscious mind that you should be prepared for the full range of emotions. You might try to structure it just with a friend or just with a lover or just with you know in a religious spiritual context or something like that or in a recreational party context. But whatever it is, you should be open to the full range of experiences. And we find that a lot of people take drugs for recreational purposes. Then when psychological stuff starts coming up of prior abuse, they try to stuff it all back down. So I think that's really detrimental to themselves. It kind of takes things that have been suppressed, but then that you've moved them up to awareness, but if you don't fully process them, then they torment you or bother you or cause anxiety. So I think it's really important to open to whatever is happening when it's happening whatever moods they are whatever. And then I guess the third and final thing maybe is just to say that it's important to do all the preparation that you can do and think about your intentions and think about what you want to do. Think about the environment. Think about who you want to be with. Try to structure it as much as you can. But once you've taken the drug, just let all that go. And then just be spontaneous about what happens, what emerges. And then once you've created the structure, but then you've freed yourself from it. You'll find that a lot of the elements of the structure that you've created will prove to be really helpful. But you want to be spontaneous and able to go in unexpected directions because psychedelics bring unexpected ideas to the surface and emotions. [Music] When people take psychedelics and things come to the surface, it's part of them. It's not really a drug experience that they're having. They're having an experience of themselves that's been mediated by this drug. And that same experience can come out through dreams. It can come out through art. It can come out through daydreaming. It can come out in any number of different ways. So people it could if they take different drugs it has a different kind of flavor or angle but it's sort of your own internal themes and so I think if people can kind of recognize that and that once you've sort of catalyze this emergence then there's so many different ways that integration process can take place but that it all involves reflection on what happened and also as honest of an inventory as you can make about what happened what you'd like to have happen on a more permanent basis from what happened during the psychedelics and then how do you in a little way start building up your capacity so that that might be more of a normal daily new capacity. So for us the way we do the MDMMA assisted psychotherapy it's during the day starts around 10:00 in the morning goes till 6:00 in the evening. It's a male female co-therapist team. We require the people to spend the night at the treatment center so that they don't distract themselves with traffic and with going back home and talking to family and friends. But then the most crucial part is the next day after they've had a relaxed morning, the therapists come back and they have multiple hours of discussions about what happened before. And the therapists have taken notes, they've done videotapes. And when somebody's experiencing psychedelics, so much is happening that you have a tendency to sort of grab on to like that was useful, I'm going to remember that. And that sort of slows down the emergence of new material when you had to grab on to stuff that was valuable from before. So the therapists are taking notes during the session and then during the integration process, you can discuss them and that can really help bring back feelings and help anchor them in your post psychedelic day. Then when people go home, we actually call them on the phone every day for a week for 5 minutes, 10 minutes, 15 minutes, half an hour, depending on what's going on with them to see how they're processing it. How is the integration process actually going? And so we have a very rigorous container for people's experiences. Um, you know, after the therapists leave, we have a night attendant come in so people are not by themselves. If they need help, they know where to get it. So, I think this integration process is really key because what we're trying to do, which is nonprofit drug development with psychedelics and marijuana, we're trying to show that these experiences have lasting benefits. It's not enough to show that these experiences are enjoyable or they're, you know, seemingly profound. It's about what are the lasting benefits. And to do that, there needs to be a lot of anchoring in the integrative process. Um, we find that people really like art, like to draw, even people that aren't good drawers, that that there's a way in which you kind of non-verbbally can get feelings out in art. Um, you know, people have written long journal entries. Um, I myself have had, you know, experiences that have transferred to dreams and then sort of mutated into new psychedelic experiences, different psychedelics than the one I took before. And so I I do have this feel for how the material comes out in different ways and it's kind of a unified sense of where it's coming from. So I think the integration is about just reflecting on what happened, but it's also about making these small tiny changes in everyday life that would support whatever new direction people wanted to go in. And that's really the the key to the integration process is putting into practice what you had learned or experienced or decided to try or that you hoped for is actually can you put that into practice even in little ways talking to your family and more open ways or whatever it happens to be. I think you know taking the courage finding some small step that you can do and then that builds your capacity your muscles for the integration process. [Music]

=== VIDEO: iwYz_voQmXE (https://youtu.be/iwYz_voQmXE) ===

Thank you. Today we've heard many stories of courage, of struggle or triumph, and of defeat, and most of all, of trauma. I'm here today to share the news that there's new hope for the treatment of trauma. PTSD, post-traumatic stress disorder, is a debilitating condition after exposure to a traumatic event or a series of traumatic events. The symptoms include re-experiencing, coming back in nightmares, intrusive thoughts during the day avoidance, people avoid reminders or triggers of the trauma, there's often emotional numbing fear, depression, and hypersensitivity, hypervigilance and all of this combines into a diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorder. More people than you might imagine actually suffer from PTSD. Six to 10% of all people will develop PTSD at some point during their lifetime and more so if you're working for human rights, or struggling against oppressive regimes or if you're a veteran. Right now, there's about one million veterans that are receiving disability payments for PTSD from the Veterans Administration at a cost of around $20 billion a year. According to the Open Global Society, a survey was conducted of human rights workers that shows that 18% of human rights workers have PTSD and another 18% suffer from symptoms a little bit short of a diagnosis. That's a staggering number. 38% of human rights workers and even more so sometimes than the people that they're trying to help are struggling with trauma and they don't have available to them the most effective treatments. Only 40 to 60% of people with PTSD respond to the currently available treatments. That's an enormous amount of suffering. The currently available treatments include primarily pharmacotherapy drugs such as Zoloft and Paxil that address the symptoms but not the root cause that require daily administration, often for months, years, or decades, or a lifetime that have significant side effects and that really don't leave people feeling much better. There's also psychotherapies, and these work well for quite a few people, but they're very difficult because they require confronting the trauma and for many people, that's too difficult, and so there's a lot of drop-outs from the currently available psychotherapies. For the last 30 plus years, I've been working to develop a new, innovative treatment for post-traumatic stress disorder, which comes from the psychedelic drug called MDMA. MDMA was invented in 1912 by Merck Pharmaceutical Company, but they didn't know what they had. They never really tested it in humans, and it wasn't until the middle 1970s when it was rediscovered in the United States by a group of therapists and psychiatrists and roughly half a million doses were used in therapeutic settings from the middle 70s to the early 80s. But MDMA escaped out of this therapeutic context and became the recreational drug probably all of us have heard about as ecstasy. And this happened during the time of Ronald Reagan and Nancy Reagan and the escalation of the drug war, so it was clear that MDMA was going to be criminalized. In the summer of 1984, the DEA moved to put MDMA in Schedule I, criminalizing all medical and recreational use and a group of advocates and myself banded together and there's a picture of me in the background, spying on the DEA before I walked in the door to take them totally by surprise and demand a hearing. At first, we thought we were winning. We got the hearing. And then, the DEA administrative law judge said we won that it should remain a therapeutic drug. But the administrator of the DEA rejected the recommendation and placed MDMA in Schedule I criminalizing all therapeutic as well as recreational use. And so, in 1986, I founded a nonprofit pharmaceutical company, the Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies to try to bring MDMA forward through science, through medicine, and focused on healing. It took 30 years for us to raise the money to do the studies to enable us to move to the FDA for what's called an end of Phase II meeting and that's where you present your data and you ask for permission to move to Phase III the final stage of research before you can make a drug into a prescription medicine and the FDA said yes. Now, I'd like to share with you one story of healing from a 37-year-old veteran, Jon Lubecky who was 12 years in the Army and the Marines, and when he came back from Iraq he was so wounded with trauma that he attempted suicide five times. The last time, he put a gun to his head he pulled the trigger, and it misfired and he finally decided to seek help. And he went to the Veterans Administration, and everything they offered him did not work for him. And out of desperation, he
volunteered for our study. And our treatment basically consists of three and a half months of treatment with a male/female co-therapy team, but only three administrations of MDMA one month apart with 12 90 minute, non-drug psychotherapy sessions three before the first MDMA session, for preparation and then three after each MDMA session, for integration. After the treatment, John wrote "I cannot emphasize how much MDMA-assisted psychotherapy has changed my life. I went from constant daily suicidal ideation, anxiety, depression, to almost nothing. The best part was this was not lifelong treatment and medication. This was only three experiences of MDMA." PTSD actually changes people's brains. People's amygdala, the part of our brain where we process fear, becomes hyperactive and activity is increased there. Where we think logically, the prefrontal cortex and put experiences in context, activity is reduced and activity is reduced in the hippocampus, where we can put things in long-term memory. MDMA changes people's brains as well, but in the opposite direction. MDMA reduces activity in the amygdala permitting us to remember traumatic experiences with less fear, so that we can process it. MDMA increases activity in the prefrontal cortex where we can store long-term memory, and it increases connectivity between the hippocampus and the amygdala so that memories that are seeming like they're never really in the past can become processed and put into the past. To give you a better sense of how MDMA actually works in therapy I'm gonna show you a two minute video from one of the therapy sessions the first therapy session of an Iraq War veteran who, after he came back, was struggling with rage. Now, this veteran, Nicholas Blackstone, has not forgotten it. This session was almost 10 years ago, and he's still PTSD-free. To give you a sense of the results that we got from our Phase II studies the placebo group, the people that received the therapy with either inactive placebo or very low dose MDMA, showed actually pretty good results. 23% of them no longer had PTSD after our treatment. That's really pretty good compared to other therapies or compared to the pharmacotherapies. However, when we added MDMA to the therapy, the results more than doubled. 56% of the people no longer had PTSD, and this is really remarkable. These are chronic, severe, treatment-resistant PTSD patients. But what's even better is that this process catalyzed a healing process that people can continue on their own. So, at the 12 month follow-up, after the last MDMA session without any more MDMA, over two thirds of the people no longer had PTSD. And of the one third that still had PTSD, many of them had significant reductions in their symptoms and if we could've gone along to a fourth or fifth session they might also have gone below the level of having PTSD. On the basis of these results, the FDA granted us breakthrough therapy. It's the most important designation for the most promising drugs and in a sense now, we are partnered with the FDA on bringing this drug to market as quickly as we can. We've also started negotiations with the European Medicines Agency. On June 12th, this was this meeting, we were given approval by the EMA to also move to Phase III. But in something that both surprised and pleased us, they appealed to use to include refugees and migrants with trauma in our studies, because that's such a problem in Europe. And now, we've also been able to obtain bipartisan support for what we're doing. We've had great coverage in the New York Times, but you may be surprised that we've also had terrific coverage in Fox News. According to a new study, soldiers with PTSD using the illegal drug known as MDMA, or ecstasy cured the condition within weeks. This is key, 'cause I don't think I've ever seen the word "cured" in a highfalutin medical journal. Usually it "significantly improves," but never cures. Now, the study was small, 26 patients, but their message is big. Jessie, did you ever think you'd be doing a pro-ectasy segment on Fox New? No, my mind's spinning right now and I haven't taken any ecstasy. Healing trauma in veterans and others is enabling us to build bridges across political divides. We're even able to overcome resistance from the War on Drugs. In essence, the War on Drugs has never been about drug abuse it's been about the persecution of minorities and it's been about the suppression of the freedom of thought, of cognitive liberty and it's also caused enormous unnecessary suffering by the suppression of research with drugs such as MDMA, marijuana, psilocybin, and many others. Fortunately, MAPS has been able to raise $27 million in donations, not investments but in donations, to start phase III research which we're going to
be starting next month. We anticipate, by 2021, that we will have MDMA available as a medicine and we'll actually be able to start using it with patients on a compassionate basis a year from now and we anticipate the same for EMA, depending on our ability to raise funding for the European research. For those heroes in the audience here, and watching, and around the world who have struggled for human rights, for freedom against oppressive regimes for whom resilience is a constant struggle the message today is that there is hope and there is healing. Thank you.

Rick Dublin is the founder and president of the multiddisciplinary association for psychedelic studies, MAPS. He has been a leading voice in advancing psychedelic research and therapy for decades. In our conversation filmed at the psychedelic science 2025 conference in Denver, he shares his vision for integrating psychedelics into mainstream culture and mental health care. We discuss how he sees the state of the movement after 40 years of activism and what the future may hold following the FDA's rejection of MDME assisted therapy approval. June 2024 uh two scientific bodies came to very different conclusions about the same subject. The Dutch state commission said that MDMA therapy is effective and safe >> while FDA made a decision to reject the the same treatment. Why do you think these two bodies came to so different conclusions? >> I'm so glad you've started with that question. Um I think there's several major reasons. First off, the um Dutch committee had real subject matter experts. The people that were on the FDA advisory committee mostly didn't know what they were talking about. Didn't know about psychedelics, didn't even know that much about therapy, knew hardly anything about PTSD. So, first off, the qualifications of the people. Secondly, the Dutch group had about a year to study the data and the FDA advisory committee had a couple weeks. But I think the most important point is that the Dutch group had to justify their findings in writing a report which meant that they had to stick to the evidence. They had to think about the evidence. Whereas all the FDA advisory committee people had to do was say, "Oh, I vote this. I vote that." They were much more influenced by the media, the FDA advisory committee, than they were by the briefing package that was given to them by the FDA. And they acknowledged multiple times that they didn't really know what was going on. Oh, we read the Washington Post that the we read the ICE all criticizing um, so the methodological problems. Um there's no good solution simple solution to the double blind problem but more importantly a big part of my dissertation which I completed in 2001 before we started all these studies was how to deal with the double blind problem and the solution that I have was different than the solution that you suggested basically it was that it should be therapy with lowd dose MDMA versus therapy with full dose MDMMA and the challenge is to find the low so to find the low dose that's high enough to cause confusion. So the challenge is what's the low dose that's not micro dose doesn't work. What's the low dose that's causing enough confusion? You're not sure whether it's the full dose, the smaller dose, but it's not such a high dose that it has therapeutic potential and then you you'll not be able to tell the difference between the two groups. So we spent 16 years trying to find the right dose. And we looked at therapy with inactive placebo, therapy with 25 milligrams, with 30 milligrams, with 40 milligs, with 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150. And in each of these situations, we give half the initial after 2 hours to to keep the plateau. And so we were sort of looking for this sweet spot of where is this dose? And what we found to our surprise was that the lower doses did cause a bit more confusion but they made people uncomfortable. So this is like the 25 um 30 40 and 50. And what it turned out is that we got better results in the people that got therapy with no MDMA than the people got therapy with lowd dose MDMA. Then the other so the example I use is like turbulence taking off in an airplane. At the beginning it's kind of turbulent. Uh but then you get high enough and it's smooth. So it's that way with low doses of things sometimes they can make you activate you. Now these are not even healthy volunteers of course. These are people with lots of PTSD. The other thing that surprised us, we did a study that um was in uh firefighters, police officers, and veterans mostly. Um and we had three different dose groups. And um one of them was around 30 milligs, one was 75, one was 125. And in this particular study, because of randomization, it's not always equal, but the group that had 125 had more comorbid depression with the PTSD than the group that had the 75. But the 75 milligram group did the best, even better than the 125 group. So even aside from this situation with the depression, we found that the 75 milligram dose group was very effective. So we didn't find this sweet spot of where to do this um control active control that was either not hurting the therapy as compared to no MDMA at all or not really helping the therapy. So I think we we hurt ourselves by wanting to be too scientific. So we went to the FDA during this 8-month process after we got approval to go to phase three. So, I knew that the double blind was going to be an issue and that we needed to come to an agreement with on FDA ahead of time and that's the whole purpose of this special uh protocol assessment process. And so, we presented that information. We said, "We'd be glad to give you blinding, but you're going to make our job easier to find a difference between the two groups because the blinding at these lower doses compromises the therapy." And then we said that the real question we think is if you can do this with therapy, why add the drug? And so you should be testing the therapy when it's the most effective. The other thing we said is that in the safety database, if we use a lowdose MDMA, we're not sure if there's serious adverse events. Are they due to this lowd dose MDMA that's making people uncomfortable or is it due to the therapy or or whatever? So when you have an inactive placebo, what you know is that whatever side effects you see are from the therapy from people with severe PTSD or moderate to severe PTSD going through the therapy. It's not because you've added a little catalyst of a drug. So you get a better safety database when you do therapy with inactive placebo. So we presented to the FDA and said you tell us what you want us to do. And then what they said is that in reality the double blind fails all the time even with SSRIs that you have the side effect profiles that the patients can recognize the the therapists often can recognize. So what the FDA said is that in acknowledgement of the um functional unblinding actually taking place, there's two main ways to reduce experimental bias that they wanted to introduce into the study. The first is something everybody does, random assignment. You know, everybody is similarly motivated. The second is this robust series of independent raiders where the raiders are blind to the condition whether somebody got MDMA or somebody got the placebo or whether they're in the control group or the treatment group and you have we had over 20 independent raiders that did monthly interrator reliability. They were randomly assigned to the next person that needed it. So we instituted this very robust system of independent raiders. Um and that that's what the the FDA agreed on. And so our problem was the people that we worked with at the FDA that agreed on this left the FDA and then new more conservative people at the Division of Psychiatry took over. Because when you make these agreements, you're not supposed to be making it with individuals. You're making with the agency. Hundreds of thousands more than 200,000 have committed suicide just veterans. So if I would have seen that it would have taken almost 40 years and we're not there, you know, it's tragic. Um, but when I started, I thought it might never work anyway. I mean, we were in the middle of this massive DEA suppression of it, but also when I started in '86, um, we were in this period where FDA was blocking all psychedelic research. There was no psychedelic research, not anywhere in the world even. So it was pretty grim when I started. And so in some ways um I'd be disappointed at the scale of the suffering that didn't need to be and that it has taken us this long. Um, one of the big problems to do research with psychedelics is how do you do a double blind study, right? you know, when you take a a powerful drug, you know you've taken it. And if you give an inactive placebo, people can tell the difference. Um, a lot of my dissertation, which I did in 2001, was how to do a double blind study with psychedelics, particularly with MDMA. And I my my solution which made sense was um to do therapy with lowd dose MDMA versus therapy with full dose MDMMA. So everybody knows they're getting MDMA. They all have the same expectations and they might not be aware though which dose you know is it a full dose is it a lower dose. So the challenge was to pick the low dose so that it's high enough to cause a certain amount of confusion but not so high that it has so so much therapeutic potential that you can't tell the difference between the groups. So for 16 years from 2000 to 2016, MAPS did a series of what are called phase 2 studies to try to figure out how to do phase 3. And we looked at therapy with no MDMA, therapy with 25 milligrams, 30 milligrams, 40, 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150. So sort of like a dose response. We did all these different doses. And what we discovered, fortunately, after I'd got my PhD, was that my theory was partially right and partially wrong. that um you know a micro dose of of of anything is not going to be very good as a placebo because you'll be able to tell. So what was surprising to us was that the lower doses 25 30 40 50 milligrams did indeed um cause a certain amount of confusion. But when you're working with PTSD patients and you get this activation from the drug, but you don't have enough uh of the fear reduction, it made people uncomfortable. So, we showed that the people that got therapy with no MDMA did better than the people that got therapy with the low doses of MDMA. They still got better, but they didn't get as much benefit. So, the the analogy is you're taking off in an airplane and there's all this turbulence at the beginning and then you get above the clouds and it's smooth sailing. So, it's it's kind of like that with with MDMA. But the part that we discovered that was very surprising was we did a study with veterans, firefighters, and police officers. And one group got 30 milligrams, one group got 75 milligrams, and one group got 125 milligrams. And in that particular study, when you it was about 26 people. And they said that there's two things that you can do to reduce experimental bias because the whole purpose of the double blind is to sort of reduce bias that you don't know what's going on and everybody just treats everybody the same. They said the first is this random assignment. What that means is everybody's similarly motivated and they will work um and the therapists don't know necessarily that so you do this random assignment. But then the second thing is that you can't have the therapist or the patients rate themselves on how well they've done compared to baseline. Even with SSRIs, you think that Prozac or various um drugs that you take that are not psychedelic, that those are easy to double blind, but they're not because when people have sexual side effects, they have other side effects and they report to their therapist what's going on, then they can tell from the side effect profile. So, the double blind fails in practice a lot. But the FDA is is saying we can't just only approve drugs where the double blind is perfect. There's we have to weigh these different things. And what we showed is that um twothirds of the people that had severe PTSD no longer had PTSD after the treatment, which was 42 hours of therapy, three MDMA sessions, one month apart, 12 90minute non-drug psychotherapy sessions. Um twothirds no longer had PTSD that got therapy plus MDMA. And those people that got therapy without MDMA, with the inactive placebo, roughly one-third no longer had PTSD. And then another um 20 roughly um 20% had what's called um clinically significant reductions of PTSD symptoms, means that their life has changed. Their symptoms are not as burdensome, but they still have PTSD. So they're called responders. So we had 88% responders, only 12% non-responders. Wow. It it was it's it's the best treatment. In the second phase three study, we we purposely moved it to moderate to severe PTSD because we didn't want the FDA to say it's only for severe PTSD. 3/4ers of the people did have severe PTSD. That's better than any of the other therapies for PTSD. And so what that demonstrated is that the therapists, even though most of them could tell the difference between whether somebody had MDMA or not, that they tried just as hard as they could to help people whether they got the MDMA or not. And people can go to some happy memories or to layers of trauma, whatever. They're not forced to just focus on the trauma. === VIDEO: 6ClPE7lLqsc (https://youtu.be/6ClPE7lLqsc) ===

There's roughly 9 to 10 million people in America that have PTSD. And there's many, many millions of them that are severe, chronic,
treatment-resistant PTSD. And what that means is that
there are a lot of people that have posttraumatic stress disorder for whom the currently
available medications and psychotherapies don't work. (gentle soothing music) - I think the world, in
general, is overwhelmed and our available treatment
options are limited and can only reach so many people. And I think psychedelics hold a promise for bringing a lot more healing to larger numbers of people. MDMA-assisted psychotherapy is unique among trauma treatments, in that it allows the participants, or the patients to access their traumas in a way that they aren't overwhelmed by their past experiences. They can address their
fear around their trauma. - I started MAPS in 1986 as a non-profit psychedelic
pharmaceutical company with the goal of taking MDMA through the regulatory system to make it into a prescription medicine. Now we're at this point where, 32 years after MAPS was started, we're about to start Phase 3. And Phase 3 is the
final stage of research to make a drug into a medicine, to prove safety and efficacy. (light piano music) - We completed six Phase 2 clinical trials. We see over time that symptoms improve after MDMA-assisted psychotherapy immediately after a few treatments, but then when we follow up 12 months later, we see that even a greater number of participants no longer meet criteria for PTSD. (light piano music) - In August of 2017, we got Breakthrough Therapy
designation from the FDA for the most promising drugs and then since that point
we've been doing fundraising and training of more therapists. We anticipate by 2021, that MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD will be available legally by prescription. - What I learned from my
experience in the MDMA training study as a participant, is that people with PTSD, and people who don't have
PTSD, aren't that different. In the right conditions, the psyche moves towards wholeness. The psyche moves towards healing. Basically what we want
to do is to mainstream psychedelic medicine
and make that available to people who have a wide range of clinical indications,
and with your help, together, we can do it. (gentle soothing music)

=== VIDEO: oUJUV9jYd98 (https://youtu.be/oUJUV9jYd98) ===

Veterans who are coming back from the war many of them feel that the psychological wounds that they are coming home with have not been successfully treated by the currently available treatments that are offered to them through the VA. They, they work for many soldiers but there are some people for whom the currently available treatments do not work and I think there's a lot of discontent by veterans who are searching for something else and MDMA-assisted psychotherapy is something that can be helpful when other treatments have failed. And what we're trying to do is work with the Veterans Administration, with the Department of Defense, so that they can integrate this as one of the tools among many that they work with vets and I think if they were to do so they would have a higher level of satisfaction and cures among the vets that they're working with. This, this study is of crucial importance to us for our negotiations and discussions with the VA and with the Department of Defense because if we can show in this small pilot study that veterans who have not been able to be helped by the currently available treatments and medicines can be assisted to lead healthier lives through MDMA therapy that, we'll be able to talk more persuasively to the VA and to the Department of Defense how this might be one of the tools that they want to add to the treatments that they offer through the formal system to the veterans that are coming back. Help complete this study Support MDMA-assisted psychotherapy research for veterans with PTSD on indiegogo
mdmaptsd.org/healingtrauma mdmaptsd.org/healingtrauma Subtitles by the Amara.org community

[Music] ever since i was 18 years old in 1972 for all of my adult life since then i've devoted my life to trying to bring back psychedelic research to become a psychedelic therapist and also to go through my own psychedelic therapy in 1986 i started maps the multi-disciplinary association for psychedelic studies as a non-profit psychedelic pharmaceutical company that would also do public education and drug policy reform because i felt that the psychedelics had been very much suppressed and mdma had just been criminalized in 85 but they had an incredible amount of therapeutic potential and i said that's impossible nothing good comes from lsd and he said no it's true and i checked that out and it was true ken kesey did write parts of one floor of the cuckoo's nest while he was under the influence of lsd so that's what really made me start to question the education that i'd gotten and led me to try lsd and mescaline and during those experiences i had this intimation of this ego dissolution and this larger world than just my own individual consciousness however part of something much bigger goes back you know billions of years and connects us up with all of life and all different kind of people that we have way more in common than separate and it felt to me like these experiences were so important to the survival of humanity and also to you know me personally and that they were so suppressed that nixon said that timothy leary was the most dangerous man in america and that made me think what does timothy leary doing right that would have nixon so upset and i felt it was a lot of this sense of how we're all connected and that that would undermine a certain kind of racism prejudice fundamentalism and so basically at 18 years old i decided to devote my life We have a panel here of some of the participants of the MDMA trials. It's going to be moderated by Emily Williams. Emily Williams, MD, is a psychiatry resident at US UCSF where she is currently conducting an analysis of the effects of MDMA on the therapeutic alliance as well as serving as a co-PI on an MDMA assisted psychotherapy for PTSD phase 3 clinical trial site. She also works as the independent clinical writer for the MAPS funded study on MDMMA for end of life anxiety in Marin California. In addition to her clinical re and research work, she is has a passion for harm reduction as and serves as a supervisor for the Zeno project which provides psychedelic harm reduction for for events and festivals. Please welcome this amazing team, this amazing panel. [Applause] Thank you all so much for joining me for this panel of MDMA study participants. I feel so honored to be sharing the stage with them as they share their stories of being uh study volunteers. Um for the format of this panel, um we're going to have each participant share about their experience as study volunteers. Um and then we'll reserve 15 minutes at the end for a question and answer uh with the audience. Um, so we'll begin with Andy Gold. So Andy Gold is a lawyer in Oakland, California. He was diagnosed with stage three colon cancer in 2004. At the recommendation of a psychiatrist, he ended up as a subject in a MAP sponsored study of MDMA assisted psychotherapy for people suffering with anxiety from life-threatening illness. His article, When the Walls Come Tumbling Down, my first MDMA assisted psychotherapy session, appeared in the winter 2016 maps bulletin. Hello. Give me give me a minute to get was used to this mic. Um, first, when I get nervous, which I'm likely to do in hearing my voice echoing back at me in a crowd like this, uh, one of two things is likely to happen. Um when I turned 50, uh right on time, uh I started getting severe sort of gastrointestinal discomfort and um it was time for my baseline colonoscopy. Um, so in a way I was in a strange way, um, when they told me I had cancer, I was almost relieved because I'd been having a lot of physical discomfort without knowing what it was. Um, it's not a pleasurable drug. Um, so it's just a horrible thing. === VIDEO: kDRIKpk3I20 (https://youtu.be/kDRIKpk3I20) ===

In just nine minutes, discover the most powerful insights from Joe Rogan's explosive talk with psychedelic pioneer Rick Doblin. From how therapy may need illegal drugs to why veterans are being denied a life-saving cure and the shocking truth that the DEA's first move was illegal, this video breaks down 10 takeaways on MDMA therapy, MAPS, drug laws, trauma, and the future of mental health. Takeaway one, psychedelics can transform mental health care. Psychedelic therapy, especially using MDMA, is proving to be a powerful new way to treat mental health issues like post-traumatic stress disorder. Rick Doblin, who has spent over 40 years researching this, explains how MDMA helps reduce fear in the brain, making it easier for people to revisit painful memories without being overwhelmed. Instead of numbing the pain like anti-depressants do, MDMA opens up a safe emotional space where people can finally talk through their trauma. According to Doblin, twothirds of the people with severe PTSD no longer qualified as having PTSD after just three sessions with MDMA plus therapy. For example, in recent studies, even veterans with decades of unresolved trauma showed major improvements after only a few months of treatment. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway two, therapy matters more than the drug alone. MDMA itself isn't a cure. It's the therapy that happens during and after the MDMA experience that drives the real change. Doin emphasizes that psychedelics work best when taken in a guided setting with trained professionals, not just for recreational use. He explains how patients go through preparation sessions followed by the MDMA experience and then multiple sessions to help process what came up. This full process is what leads to long-term healing. For example, in some ketamine clinics that skip the therapy and just give the drug, people may feel temporary relief but don't actually deal with the root of their problems. That's why integration therapy is essential. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway three. MAPS is leading psychedelic research. The Multidisciplinary Association for Psychedelic Studies, better known as MAPS, was founded in 1986 by Rick Doblin. It is a nonprofit that has spent decades fighting to bring psychedelic assisted therapy into mainstream medicine. MAPS runs clinical trials, trains therapists, and works with governments to make MDMA a legal treatment. It's not a company that wants to make billions from patents, but a mission-driven group that wants MDMA to become a generic drug so it stays affordable. For example, MAP supported a public benefit corporation called Lycos Therapeutics to handle the drug development part while continuing its nonprofit mission separately. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway four, the war on drugs caused real damage. Doin points out that the criminalization of MDMA in 1985 wasn't based on solid science, but was more about political fear during the Reagan era drug panic. He says the first people arrested for MDMA had their charges dropped because the DEA didn't even have the legal authority to ban it. Still, the damage was done. Research was shut down, therapists were raided, and people who could have been helped were left to suffer. If MDMA hadn't been made illegal, Dolin says we could have saved thousands of lives, especially among veterans. For example, one tragic result is that veteran suicide rates have stayed high while potential therapies remain locked away due to bad laws. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway five. Political support for psychedelics is growing. Even conservative leaders are starting to change their views on psychedelics. Former Texas Governor Rick Perry, a Republican, is now a vocal supporter of Ibeane Research, another powerful psychedelic. This shift happened after he heard about dramatic healing stories from veterans like Congressman Morgan Latrell, who suffered from severe trauma and found real recovery through psychedelic therapy. Doblin explains that psychedelics have found rare bipartisan support in Washington because the evidence is so strong and the suffering is so real. For example, Congress recently gave $10 million to fund MDMA therapy research for active duty soldiers through the Department of Defense. We're halfway through the video. Thanks for sticking with us. If you're enjoying it, hit the thumbs up and share it in your WhatsApp groups. If you'd like to support us, please tap the thanks button below. It helps us keep making great content. Drop a comment and don't forget to hit subscribe for more. Now, let's continue with the video. Takeaway six, psychedelics can help with end of life anxiety. One of the lesserk known uses of psychedelics is helping people who are dying deal with fear, sadness, and unresolved emotions. Doin talks about how MDMA and psilocybin have been used in studies with terminal cancer patients. These people often feel trapped by anxiety and depression, and psychedelics have helped them come to peace with the idea of death. He explains that these treatments don't just help the patient, but also their loved ones. For example, a study at Sunstone Therapy Center gave MDMA to both the cancer patient and their partner, leading to deep emotional healing for both. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway seven, group therapy could make treatments cheaper. One of the challenges of psychedelic therapy is the cost. A full course of MDMA assisted therapy includes dozens of hours with two trained therapists, which is expensive. Doblin shares how new studies are testing group therapy models to lower the cost and reach more people. In some trials, people get individual sessions first and then go through MDMA experiences in a group setting. They then meet together to share and process what happened. For example, in Ukraine and Israel, where trauma is widespread, researchers are testing this model to support war survivors with limited resources. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway eight, personal stories shape public acceptance. Doblin's own journey helps explain why he's so committed to this cause. He first tried LSD when he was 17 and said it felt like the right of passage he never got from his bar mitzvah. He wanted to understand himself and the world better and psychedelics gave him that chance. He also talks about the emotional power of visiting a cemetery in Ukraine, seeing the graves of young people killed in war, and realizing that healing their families might start with these treatments. For example, he's now working on starting psychedelic therapy programs in places like Lebanon and Ukraine, where trauma is overwhelming and few tools exist to treat it. Now, let's move to the next takeaway. Takeaway nine, prohibition creates more harm than good. The drug war has failed not just by blocking healing treatments, but by pushing people into dangerous situations. Doblin and Rogan talk about how criminalizing substances like marijuana or MDMA actually pushes them into black markets controlled by cartels. They point out how legal alcohol causes more damage than most illegal drugs, but is still socially accepted. Doblin explains how people who need help are often afraid to seek treatment because they fear being arrested or losing their kids. For example, pregnant women addicted to drugs often avoid hospitals because they worry their babies will be taken away, which leads to worse outcomes for both mother and child. Now, let's move to the last takeaway. Takeaway 10. Psychedelics can reshape society's outlook. At the end of the podcast, the conversation turns toward the bigger picture. Doblin and Rogan both believe that psychedelics could help shift society toward more empathy, understanding, and self-awareness. These substances are not just about fixing mental illness, but can also help everyday people gain new perspective on life, relationships, and purpose. Rogan says, "Everyone needs a little help. I'm not saying you're coming back from Afghanistan, but everyone could use a little pickme up, a fresh perspective." For example, in Europe, cultures that introduce young people to wine in responsible ways have far fewer problems with alcohol than the United States, which treats it as something forbidden. The same principle could apply to psychedelics if done responsibly. Here is a brief introduction about Rick Doblin. Rick Doblin is the founder and president of the multiddisciplinary association for psychedelic studies known as MAPS, a nonprofit organization working to make psychedelic assisted therapy legally available. He holds a PhD in public policy and has spent over four decades advocating for the scientific and medical use of substances like MDMA to treat trauma and mental illness. Thank you for watching. If you'd like to support this channel, hit the thanks button below. It really helps us keep going. If you enjoyed this summary, please leave a like and share it in your WhatsApp groups. To join discussion about this video, drop a comment below. And for more videos like this, hit the subscribe button below.

And the remarkable thing that we've learned about memory is that when you remember something, you have to reconsolidate the memory. You're basically recreating the memory. So that if this memory is linked to fear and under the influence of MDMA, you can bring back the memory but the fear response is dampened down. Then when you reconsolidate the memory, it doesn't have that fear to it and you've been able to put it in context as then and not now. Well, it's certainly understandable that people don't necessarily realize that this could be a a potential therapeutic tool. A lot of people don't know the history. Most people don't understand that MDMA really began as a therapy drug in the um middle 70s and early 80s. And then from that some people realized that they could make a lot of money selling it in more uh recreational settings and more public settings. And so it was just inevitable that there would be a crackdown.
